[[RunningHead]]

Heinz Kohut and the Psychology of the Self (Makers of Modern Psychotherapy)

Allen M. Siegel. New York: Routledge, 1996, 226 pp., $69.95, hardcover; $24.99, paperback.

Todd Essig

Todd Essig

59 West 12th Street
New York, NY 10011
Fax: 212-563-9666
E-mail: Tessig@psychoanalysis.net

Todd Essig   Todd Essig, faculty and member of the Council of Fellows, William Alanson White Institute for Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy, New York; Director, The Psychoanalytic Connection.

In this personable distillation of Heinz Kohut's life's work, Allen Siegel attempts “to convey the evolution of Kohut's ideas so that students of psychoanalytic psychotherapy can benefit from his erudition” (p. 192). He takes the reader through a chronological tour of Kohut's main writings, and does so with such conspicuous clarity that his fingerprints can rarely be found smudging the image of Kohut's thought. In fact, with the exception of a short concluding chapter and several excellent clinical examples of his own that clearly illustrate the value of Kohut's work for the practice of psychodynamic psychotherapy, Siegel's voice is rarely present. The book also includes a lively biographical chapter written by Ernest Wolf. While I found myself wanting to hear a bit more from Siegel himself, especially additional examples of how he has used Kohut's psychoanalytic writing in his psychotherapy practice, the author is to be commended for the clarity of his exposition throughout the work.

The book is not specifically intended for psychoanalysts, and while it may have a place in the early stages of a psychoanalytic curriculum, it is expressly intended for other mental health professionals and for students with an interest in psychodynamic thought. In addition to presenting “Kohut's ideas in a clear and understandable way so that psychotherapists of all inclinations can use his conceptualizations to inform their work” (p. 2)

[[RunningHead]]

Why Did Freud Reject God? A Psychodynamic Interpretation

Ana-Maria Rizzuto. New Haven: Yale University Press, 1998, 256 pp., $35.00

Janice Halpern

Janice Halpern

I St. Clair Avenue East
Suite 601
Toronto, Ontario M4T 2V7
Canada
Fax: 416-781-2515
E-mail: Jr_halpern@hotmail.com

Janice Halpern  

Expanding psychoanalysis beyond Freud is always a delicate endeavor. One method, used by the early object relations theorists, is to cautiously expand the theory while declaring allegiance to Freud. A more recent approach, used by Stolorow and Atwood (1978) to challenge Freud's

1009

theory of psychosexual development, is to delve into Freud's personal psychodynamics in order to identify their influence on his theory. Psychoanalysts interested in the psychology of religion have sought to advance the field beyond Freud in comparable ways. Loewald, Meissner, Rizzuto, and Spero have all respectfully broadened Freud's foray into the psychology of religion, acknowledging and expanding on the kernels of truth buried beneath his antireligious bias. In her new book. Why Did Freud Reject God?, Rizzuto takes the second approach, putting Freud once again on the couch. In so doing, she exposes the biographical influences that may have limited the founder of psychoanalysis. One result of this is to give license to present-day practitioners who would extend their thinking into areas Freud never considered.

In posing her seemingly simple question, Rizzuto stretches the reader far beyond Freud. With it, she introduces the ideas that rejecting God is a psychological position it behooves us to understand, that perhaps other options were open to Freud (such as accepting God), and indeed that God may exist as an entity to accept or reject. In answering the question, she provides an analysis of Freud's atheism, the analysis itself serving to remind us that atheism is a choice, though a largely unconscious one.

In her previous book, The Birth of the Living God: A Psychoanalytic Study, Rizzuto (1979) unfolded an object relations approach to understanding the development of internal God representations. In the first section of this new book she examines the God representation that evolved from Freud's relationship with his father. In this, she follows Meissner (1984). Her examination of Freud's antiquities collection and his relationship to it supports this hypothesis. Rizzuto, like others, recognizes the antiquities collection as a symptom and sees the compulsive need to collect these primitive god figurines, a need that arose after the death of Freud's father, as an unconscious attempt to revive a connection with him. These figurines were chosen to represent the father because they reminded Freud of the illustrations in the Philippson Bible that father and son lovingly studied together. Thus, Rizzuto adds weight to Meissner's contention of the link in Freud's mind between his father and the God of his father. In her compassionate portrayal of their complex relationship we recognize the possibility that Freud developed a God representation, through his relationship with his father, of an initially exciting but ultimately disappointing being.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Celebration and Innovation

Arnold Richards

Arnold Richards

Arnold Richards  

As the year 2000 draws to a close, we at JAPA mark the end of a year of celebration with a second special fall issue. The title of this double issue, Psychoanalysis, Development, and the Life Cycle, is a fitting one for our closing tribute to the new millennium and the first hundred years of our discipline. Fittingly too, this issue is dedicated to Sylvia Brody, whose fifty years of psychoanalytic research and practice have added so much to our understanding of development in infancy and beyond, and whose spirit will continue to inspire us through the new annual Award that her generosity has made possible. In her honor this issue contains a wealth of studies of developmental theory and its clinical implications, with particular attention to Margaret Mahler's seminal work on separation-individuation and John Bowlby's equally fundamental vision of attachment theory. We think our readers will find the broad range of contributions informative, controversial, and an advance for our field.

A last note: The papers on attachment in the following section (pp. 1045-1195) were originally presented at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December 20, 1997. This is the first time, I believe, that JAPA has presented a panel in its entirety. This one was entitled “Attachment: Research, Theory, Application.” It was chaired by Joseph Lichtenberg. The panelists were Judith F. Chused (discussant), Peter Fonagy, Mary Main, Stephen Seligman (reporter), and Arietta Slade. JAPA is pleased to present this important panel complete, with all of its component papers in revised and expanded form.*

Main's original panel presentation has been expanded into a pair of companion papers, one of which was coauthored by Erik Hesse.1053

responseSet.title: Future and Potentiality in the Psychoanalytic Process
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Future and Potentiality in the Psychoanalytic Process

Peter Schou

Peter Schou

6214 Drexel Avenue
Los Angeles, CA 90048
E-mail: Pschou@aol.com

Peter Schou  

A perspective is delineated on the dimension of the future in the psychoanalytic situation. Clinical manifestations are presented of the tension between actuality and potentiality that characterizes the treatment situation. This tension, an aspect of the intersubjective field that exists between patient and analyst, involves the analyst's hopes, expectations, anticipations, sense of purpose, and therapeutic intent, facets of the analyst's subjectivity that affect the clinical process. The question of the patient's individuality and autonomy is raised in the context of the notion of the “true self.” To understand potentiality in the clinical situation, it is argued, the intersubjective emphasis on the inevitable mutual influence between analyst and patient must be complemented by a view of the self as emerging from within and gaining coherence through the unfolding of inherent dispositions and potentialities.

[[RunningHead]]

The Cultural Origins of Human Cognition

Michael Tomasello Cambridge: Harvard University Press, 2000, 248 pp., $29.95.

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz

180 North Michigan Avenue
Chicago, IL 60601
Fax: 312-751-9525
E-mail: Belitowitz@aol.com

Bonnie Litowitz   Faculty, Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis; Associate Professor, Department of Psychiatry, Rush Medical School

This book stakes out a position on the question of what makes us human; specifically, how much to grant to biology and how much to culture. Throughout the ages, answers to this question have sought a

1641

balance between the two poles of the nature-nurture dichotomy, with resulting cycles during which one pole and then the other is in ascendance for a while. Currently there is a great deal of renewed interest in the biological dimensions of our nature, influenced by scientific and technological advances in fields such as genetics, neuroscience, and psychopharmacology. With its beginnings firmly rooted in our biology, psychoanalysis is finding the current zeitgeist very compatible (as evidenced by the new journal Neuro-psychoanalysis).

As is clear from its title, this book emphasizes the cultural dimensions, or “sociogenesis.” As such, it offers psychoanalytic readers an alternative perspective, with the further twist that it recasts the nature-nurture dichotomy in terms of primate studies: what do we share with other primates, on the one hand, and what are the effects of our shared history with conspecifics, on the other? Tomasello's position is that we share a great deal with other primates, such as our capacities for: spatial orientation; classification and permanence of objects; and recognizing conspecifics and their affiliative and dominance relations (e.g., the horizontal and vertical patterns of kinship in humans). Furthermore, with our closet primate relatives even more is shared, such as the abilities to “predict the behavior of individuals based on such things as their emotional state and their direction of locomotion; use many types of social and communicative strategies to out-compete groupmates for valued resources; cooperate with conspecifics, in problem-solving tasks and in forming social coalitions and alliances; [and] engage in various forms of social learning in which [we] learn valuable things from conspecifics” (p. 17).

Since the author is codirector of the Max Planck Institute for Evolutionary Anthropology and coauthor of Primate Cognition, he is a welcome and knowledgeable guide through the literature on primate studies, reports of which are often heavily biased toward on end or the other of the nature-nurture dichotomy. For example, a study of potato-washing among Japanese macaque monkeys is often presented as evidence for “cultural transmission” among primates. Tomasello's careful reading of the data, however, makes clear that potato-washing is “less usual a behavior for monkeys than was originally thought,” and that “the spread of the behavior was relatively slow”—that is, “the rate of spread did not increase as the number of users increased” (p. 27). A slow rate indicates individual shaping of already present behaviors (washing sand off food)

[[RunningHead]]

Intimate Attachments: Toward a New Self Psychology

Morton Shane Estelle Shane Mary and Gales. New York: Guilford Press, 1997, 242 pp., $35.00.

Ruth Gruenthal

Ruth Gruenthal

300 West End Avenue
New York, NY 10023
E-mail: RuthG26@aol.com

Ruth Gruenthal   Ruth Gruenthal, Training Analyst, Senior Supervisor, and faculty, Psychoanalytic Training Institute, Postgraduate Center for Mental Health, New York: Supervisor and faculty, New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy; Supervisor, National Institute for the Psychotherapies, New York.

This complex, comprehensive, and clinically oriented work contributes a distinctive theoretical position to the diversity of perspectives encompassed by contemporary self psychology. Writing in a cogent, eloquent, and accessible style, the authors grapple with a wide range of theoretical and clinical issues that are of concern not only to self psychology but to contemporary psychoanalysis as well. They consider such currently debated questions as the mind-body problem, the place of reality in theory and practice, conceptions of transference and countertransference, the role of the analyst's subjectivity in therapeutic action, and the setting of boundaries in a “nontraditional” approach. The authors' primary focus is on delineating their vision of psychoanalytic change. A particular feature of Intimate Attachments is the authors' use of contemporary metatheory to guide the organization of their theoretical framework. A brief review, taking up selected aspects, will inevitably fail to do justice to the impressively broad scope of this work.

The authors build on an integration of constructs extracted from key theoretical and clinical assumptions of self psychology (Kohut and contemporary contributions) and key ideas of attachment theory (Bowlby and more recent researchers). Recognizing the limitations of Kohut's one-person psychology, they aim to create a model that takes the experience of the other as other, and of relatedness to that other, into account (a two-person psychology view). They use the findings of attachment studies, and of infant and brain research, to support and further inform their integrative endeavor. Their use of contributions from related disciplines stands in contrast to the position of Kohut, who delimited the domain of psychoanalysis to data accessible to introspection and empathy as the method of observation.

336

[[RunningHead]]

The Elusive Human Subject

Roger Kennedy. London: Free Association Books, 1998, vii + 226 pp., $22.50 paperback

Stuart Pizer

Stuart Pizer

152 Brattle Street
Cambridge, MA 02138
Fax: 617–354–0662

Stuart Pizer   President-elect, Supervising Analyst, and faculty, Massachusetts Institute for Psychoanalysis; faculty, Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California; Clinical Instructor in Psychology, Harvard Medical School

Contemporary psychoanalytic discourse has directed considerable attention to examining and defining the analytic subject (Ogden 1994), the subjectivity of patient or analyst (Hoffman 1983; Aron 1996; Bollas 1989; Renik 1993), the intersubjective context of being (Stolorow and Atwood 1992), and the state, or process, of mutual recognition or “intersubjectivity” (Benjamin 1995). Several authors have in the past decade ventured a model of the analytic subject—though tending not to fuss with the distinction between subject (or subjectivity) and self—as multiply constituted (Bromberg 1996; Mitchell 1993; Davies 1996; Pizer 1996). The human subject emerges in current literature as paradoxically both centered and decentered, singular and multiple, private and interdependent—a creature who dwells in both a monadic psychic reality and a dyadic potential space, and whose truths, tales, and transformations are relationally negotiated (Bromberg 1995; Pizer 1998) or constructed (Gergen 1994).

Roger Kennedy contributes a scholarly and compendious volume in which he inquires into the location of the “human subject” and advocates a “subject relations” theory for psychoanalysis. Kennedy argues that the human subject is “elusive” because it is without center or stasis, and cannot be conceived without recognition of context, process, and dynamics—that is, the interactive social field. For Kennedy there is no subject in the absence of other subjects, indeed a network of other subjects. As he writes, “there is no one centre in this network; rather I would conceive of a series of ‘contact points,’ where subjects meet one another” (p. 7). Kennedy goes on to assert that “the organization that involves the subject, that I shall call the ‘subjective organization,’ goes beyond that of the individual as such and includes the realm of the social, and thus cuts into what can be seen mistakenly as two separate realms, the individual and the social” (p. 8). Kennedy argues that psychoanalysis is particularly suited to provide detailed descriptions of subjective states but that the wider social perspective requires the insights provided by sociology. Kennedy also includes in his book the perspectives of anthropology, social psychology, neuroscience, and philosophy.

[[RunningHead]]

The Therapeutic Alliance

Steven T. Levy. Madison, CT: International Universities Press, 1999, 155 pp., $27.50

Antoine Hani

Antoine Hani

5480 Wisconsin Avenue #1619
Chevy Chase, MD 20815
Fax: 301–365–7927
E-mail: Athani@erols.com

Antoine Hani   Training and Supervising Analyst, Washington Psychoanalytic Society and Institute

Debates about the therapeutic alliance will continue into the next century, not just as an academic exercise for those in training but because

653

controversy still exists among leading analysts. In The Therapeutic Alliance Steven T. Levy, who moderated the American Psychoanalytic Association's 1993 and 1994 Clinician's Program Seminars on the subject, has collected and commented on the presentations by Sandler Abend, Axel Hoffer, Theodore Jacobs, Judith Chused, Gerald Adler, Owen Renik, and Morton Shane. The key issue remains whether it is useful to conceptualize the therapeutic alliance as distinct from the transference or whether doing so divides the conscious and unconscious aspects of the analyst-patient relationship between the rational aspects of the alliance and the irrational aspects of the transference. Abend, who holds a classical position, stresses the importance of the analysis of the transference and sees all the patient's reactions to the analyst as transferential. He does not distinguish between the analyst as a “real” object and as a transference object. Yet he cannot fail to acknowledge the existence of the alliance as compelling, even as he tries to repudiate it. It is a truism that classical analysts focus on interpreting the unconscious meanings of analyst-patient interactions. Abend concedes that most analysts who use the therapeutic alliance as an essential technical tool know that there are transference aspects to the alliance that must be interpreted, particularly when they function as resistance to the analytic work. He does not, however, find it possible to integrate the concept of the alliance with the classical approach.

Hoffer also advocates a unitary view of the analytic relationship and argues that the concept of the alliance splits off the rational aspects of the relationship from the emotional and irrational aspects of the transference. Like Abend, Hoffer is suspicious of the alliance because it seems to uphold a rational bias, seemingly dismissing the crucial importance of affect as expressed in the transference. For Hoffer, the analyst should focus on the elucidation (not the resolution) of conflict via free association and should not take sides in the conflict or entertain any therapeutic intent. Hoffer believes that patients are motivated by a desire to be known and to discover their inner self with an analyst who is genuinely nonjudgmental and respectful, and who is motivated by “an honest desire to know the patient as deeply as the patient will allow.” Hoffer's respectful, empathic neutrality would seem conducive to the creation of the kind of holding and growth-promoting environment that Leo Stone (1961) considered the necessary basis for a therapeutic alliance.

Work and its Inhibitions: Psychoanalytic Essays

Charles W. Socarides and Selma Kramer. Madison, CT: International Universities Press, 1997, 276 pp., $42.50.

Melvin Singer

Melvin Singer

906 Centennial Road
Narberth, Penn Valley, PA 19072
Fax: 610-664-3575

Melvin Singer  

On approaching a review of this book, I felt a temporary wave of work inhibition myself. Reviewing an entire book on work felt like too much work, and not enough pleasure. I anticipated a book about the failure of neutralization or sublimation, and of identifications, especially around superego conflicts regarding oedipal struggles and castration issues. I was pleasantly surprised to find it is much, much more—a truly updated look, circa the 1990s, at the vicissitudes of work inhibitions, using all of the many paradigms currently available. Excellent clinical vignettes bring the effort to life. There is even an exceptionally powerful and poignant autobiographical paper by Lucy Daniels Inman that recounts her own work inhibition and personal analysis with practically no holds barred. Her attempt to avoid the conflicts of confidentiality is a gift to the reader.

Freud's famous dictum “lieben und arbeiten,” to love and to work, taken as a single criterion for psychic health, is the book's basic premise, to which most of the contributors adhere. That is, all but John Gedo, of course, but who better to play the devil's advocate, arguing that play without work, in the right circumstances, can in fact be very healthy—especially if one is fortunate enough to be in that special category, a status he himself now enjoys, of the “rentier,” or the category of those born into wealth and lineage whose class-dictated activity is leisure marked by refinement and decorum. Gedo's gritty-eyed, if not tongue-in-cheek, observations suggest that people in these categories don't necessarily suffer if they fail to abide by Freud's dictum. Perhaps if we all had careers as distinguished as Gedo's behind us, we could afford to rest on our laurels.

Many of the contributors here compare the components of work to play, which may be considered a developmental antecedent of work.

Work and the Evolving Self

Steven D. Axelrod. The Analytic Press: Hillsdale, NJ, 1999, 171 pp., $29.95.

Barbara Fajardo

Barbara Fajardo

122 S. Michigan Avenue
Chicago, IL 60603
Fax: 312-922-5656
E-mail: Fajardo@mcs.net

Barbara Fajardo  

This book takes a psychoanalytic perspective on work and its significance for pathology, treatment, and individual development. The book begins with a review of the psychoanalytic literature pertaining to these subjects, including theories the author will later invoke to justify his clinical strategies. For the most part this is a clinical book, with many case vignettes illustrating the author's thesis that the psychoanalytic therapist should be aggressive in taking every opportunity to identify and intervene in patients' work-related problems. Axelrod claims his thesis is a much-needed corrective to the tendency among psychoanalytic clinicians to dismiss the centrality of the work experience in the life and pathology of their patients.

In essence, Axelrod's clinical approach is to focus on the “reality” of the patient's work situation and on the significance of the patient's past as it determines the nature of present-day problems regarding work. Doing so tends to position the therapist to adopt a strategy, either supportive or authoritative and directive. While some therapists and vocational counselors may find these strategies useful, they may be of less interest to those with a more psychoanalytic clinical orientation.

663

[[RunningHead]]

Psychotherapy and Confidentiality: Testimonial Privileged Communication, Breach of Confidentiality, and Reporting Duties.

Ralph Slovenko. Springfield, IL: Charles C Thomas, 1998, 640 pp., $80.00.

Paul Mosher

Paul Mosher

6 Woodlawn Avenue
Albany, NY 12208

Paul Mosher  

We psychoanalysts have implicitly so taken confidentiality for granted in our work that for much of our history the topic has been deemed hardly worthy of comment. In fact, before the year 1960 the word confidentiality fails to appear a single time in the title, text, or bibliography of any article in our major journals (Archive I, 1996). And yet, because psychoanalysis has such a stringent requirement for “the strictest discretion” (Freud 1940), our ethical and technical insistence on protecting our patients and the treatment situation has had ramifications that have influenced the development of law applying to various forms of psychotherapy, as well as the ethics and practices of other groups of psychotherapists.

In the 1960s attention to the subject of patients' privacy began to increase as the U.S. health care system began to feel the effects of increasing litigiousness, third-party insurance coverage, Cold War security issues, and governmental and other bureaucratic involvement in financing and regulation. In 1960 an influential organization in American psychiatry, The Group for the Advancement of Psychiatry, suggested that the relationship between patient and psychiatrist should be protected from legal discovery by the establishment of what was then called a psychiatrist-patient privilege. In 1966 Ralph Slovenko, a Senior Assistant District Attorney in New Orleans, and Gene Usdin, a Tulane psychiatrist, published Psychotherapy Confidentiality and Privileged Communication. In a preface to that book, a U.S. district judge called it “the first serious attempt that has been made to expose the whole problem of privilege created by the psychotherapist-patient relationship.

665

[[RunningHead]]

The Merchant of Venice

David Millar

David Millar

120 Belsize Lane
London NW3 5BA
Fax: +0171–447–3745
England

David Millar  

July 27, 1999. Freud recognized guilt not only as essential to civilization but as its greatest problem. We cannot find our own authority without in the end taking it from those who give us life. Guilt inevitably is closely linked to the experience of loss. Melanie Klein showed that guilt becomes more bearable through growth of love and capacity to repair, while making even clearer the terrifying nature of primitive persecutory guilt and its relation to destructiveness. The projection into another group of this dark edge, suffused with sadism, badness, inferiority, and evil, makes for a sadomasochistic bond that may continue over many generations. Curiosity is attenuated as certain realities become too dangerous to get to know. A capacity for empathic identification with members of the other group is damaged. Attempts to limit or repair damage tend to be repeatedly undermined. Hatred for whatever wrongs the group might actually be responsible for becomes fused into a hatred for existing, which crucially is often normalized socially and politically. Hath not a Jew eyes? Hath not a Jew hands, organs, dimensions, senses, affections, passions? No matter, if extermination and eradication have become the good.

A few weeks before Fleisher's article and Rebecca Bach's commentary appeared in this journal, a remarkable new production of The Merchant of Venice, directed by Trevor Nunn, opened at London's Royal National Theatre. Stimulated by it, my own thoughts on the structure of Shakespeare's play show both similarities and differences when compared with the interesting points made by Fleisher and Bach. The play opens with Antonio's melancholia, soon followed by Bassanio's determination to resolve his debts, find love, have it all, by winning Portia, a beautiful, intelligent heiress. To put on a show, Bassanio turns to his merchant friend and admirer, his other half, who is so besotted with him that he can deny him nothing, The other side of this idealization is the sadomasochism reflected in the background group relations.

[[RunningHead]]

A Return to Basics

Psychoanalytic Conceptualization of Motivation

Alan Kindler

Alan Kindler

Medical Arts Building
Suite 433
170 St. George Street
Toronto, Ontario M5R 2M8
Canada
Fax: 416-920-2483
E-mail: Kindler@istar.ca

Alan Kindler  

Joseph Lichtenberg explained in his introductory remarks that the panel's title, “A Return to Basics,” was meant to address the waning interest in what once was a cornerstone of psychoanalysis, the construction of theories of motivation, and a corresponding drift toward theories of more immediate clinical relevance. The challenge faced by the panel and the audience, he said, was to determine any patterns that might emerge from the presentations suggesting that the theory of motivation remains central to the psychoanalytic enterprise. Lichtenberg briefly traced the history of psychoanalytic theories of motivation from Freud through the ego psychologists and object relations theorists to our postmodern preoccupation with multiplicity and uncertainty, and the current influence of theories of intersubjectivity, attachment, social constructivism, feminism, and the analyst's use of self.

The panel was organized as follows: Murray Peskin and Adrienne Harris would present position papers that Owen Renik and Jay Greenberg would then discuss. Then Renik and Greenberg's would present papers to be discussed by Peskin and Harris. General discussion would follow.

Peskin proposed in his position paper that the reconsideration of motivation, a fundamental concept in psychoanalytic theory, engages us with basic postulates of our field and draws us to its borders with neighboring sciences and their elaborations of the design, structure, and function of the mind.

Panel held at the Spring Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Toronto, May 30, 1998. Panelists: Joseph Lichtenberg (chair), Murray Peskin, Adrienne Harris, Owen Renik, Jay Greenberg.

901

[[RunningHead]]

Ideas and Identities: The Life and Work of Erik Erikson

Edited by Robert S. Wallerstein and Leo Goldberger. Madison, CT: International Universities Press, 1998, xii + 411 pp., $65.00.

Daniel Schwartz

Daniel Schwartz

Stockbridge, MA 01262
Fax: 413-298-4020

Daniel Schwartz  

This volume is an homage to the work of Erik H. Erikson. It is an outgrowth of a celebration of Erikson's work and life held in San Francisco on January 15, 1995, a year after the great analyst's death. It collects in one volume tributes to Erikson (including one from President Bill Clinton), four famous papers by this master of scientific writing, and articles by experts attempting to assay his multifaceted, often seminal contributions. No small, task, since these contributions include significant work in the fields of history, biography, sociology, philosophy, cultural studies, religion, human development, and of course psychoanalysis.

An essay by editor Robert Wallerstein, organizer of the San Francisco celebration, sets the stage.

962

[[RunningHead]]

Kohut, Loewald, and the Postmoderns: A Comparative Study of Self and Relationship

Judith Guss Teicholz. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 1999, xxvii + 287 pp., $47.50

Judith Vida

Judith Vida

301 South Fair Oaks Avenue, #406A
Pasadena, CA 91105
Fax: 818-353-5837

Judith Vida  

Psychoanalytic discourse, writes Judith Guss Teicholz in her introduction, “seems to be accelerating. There is a proliferation of journals, symposia, and viewpoints, some establishing dramatic differences from mainstream theory, others suggesting only subtle shadings of difference. The loss of a singular authority in psychoanalysis opens the possibility for many authoritative voices” (p. xxvi). With this book, Teicholz realizes an ambition to be one of those voices, even if “authoritative” for her will occur at the level of “compare and contrast” commentary. Her writing is lively and readable, and her scholarship is energetic, thorough, and even erudite in the precise areas of her interest.

The book grew out of Teicholz's effort to understand how it was that “theories [specifically Kohut's and Loewald's] that were considered heretical or revolutionary as recently as 1977 gradually shifted position such that they have now come to be seen … as outdated” (p. xvi).

Teicholz uses her first chapter “to argue that there have been two partial and overlapping psychoanalytic revolutions in late 20th century theory and practice, with Kohut and Loewald spearheading the one and my selected group of postmoderns (Lewis Aron, Jessica Benjamin, Irwin Z. Hoffman, Stephen A. Mitchell, and Owen Renik) spearheading the other.

979

[[RunningHead]]

The Analyst and the Working Alliance: The Reemergence of Convention in Psychoanalysis

Heinrich Deserno. Madison, CT: International Universities Press, 1998, xii + 167 pp., $30.00

Leonard Horwitz

Leonard Horwitz

P. O. Box 829
Topeka, KS 66601
Fax: 785-272-9577
E-mail: Horwitl@menninger.edu

Leonard Horwitz  

Ever since Greenson introduced and elaborated his concept of the working alliance, analysts have debated its validity and usefulness. Some have found his groundbreaking idea that “the working alliance deserves to be considered a full and equal partner to the transference neurosis in the patient-therapist relationship” (1967, p. 101) to be a highly important and useful contribution. Others have regarded it as a

997

threat to the integrity of the transference concept, insofar as the patient's “rational, non-neurotic rapport” with the analyst is necessarily infused with transference elements. Deserno clearly stands with the latter group in contending that the working alliance can be the Trojan horse in which the analyst conceals and subtly conveys his expectation that the patient comply and cooperate. In fact, Deserno makes a significant contribution to the debate by asserting that the working alliance may become a vehicle for the analyst to unconsciously and unwittingly demand that the patient follow certain social conventions in the analytic situation rather than adhere to the basic rule of free association. Despite the analyst's conscious wish to establish and ambience in which polite conventional expression is set aside in favor of uncovering the patient's neurotic conflicts, the analyst's anxieties, shame, and revulsion might lead to the desire to stifle such freedom. And, claims Deserno, the analyst's use of the working alliance concept can be the chief culprit in enabling the enactment of such unproductive countertransference-based demands.

The author's main thesis is that analysts should assiduously refrain from insisting that the patient comply with the basic rule. Rather, analysts should be constantly alert to the trap of becoming irritated when the patient stubbornly withholds or is uncooperative. His view is that the analyst must work toward interpreting the patient's transference resistance. To think in terms of a weak or ruptured alliance only leads to efforts at rational appeal when the basic issue is the enactment of a transference withholding.

[[RunningHead]]

The Mapmaker's Dilemma

Introduction to Panels

Lawrence Friedman

Lawrence Friedman

129B East 71st Street
New York, NY 10021
Fax: 212-734-3399

Lawrence Friedman  

Most analysts of the past would have felt quite at home with the discussions assembled here. To be sure, they would have been shocked by our panelists' skepticism about objective truth and the cheerful confession that they regularly distort and contaminate the scene. Many analysts of an earlier age would have raised an eyebrow, along with Spillius (Countertransference Panel), at present-day analysts' preoccupation with their own psychology. But for the most part, analysts of any time or place would recognize these topics as ones they, too, have wrestled with. What would have surprised our analytic forebears, I think, is the new interest in what I would call the problem of the continuum, under which rubric I include the various problems arising from the fluid quality of mental processes.

The mind has at least a surface unity and spreads itself out smoothly over time. It is never easy to justify breaking it up into parts, as we must do in order to think and talk about it. When Freud looked beyond easily delineated, physical seduction, beyond symbols frozen in time, beyond dreams that have a beginning and end, and beyond traumatic memories with borders around them, he came face to face with a fluctuating, seamless personal reality structured by protean wishes. Gazing on that full stream of mind, he regretfully noted how much harder it is to demonstrate transference manifestations, diffused throughout behavior, than it is to point to dream symbols specified by the patient (1905, p.116) But Freud was not unprepared—this was not his first encounter with the continuum. Freud's lasting contribution to aphasiology (1891) was made possible by his appreciation of the interconnectedness of cognition where others had seen only pieces and places. When it came time to build a theory of the mind, he was careful not to take the separation of structures too literally.

[[RunningHead]]

Commentary

Robert Michels

Robert Michels

Robert Michels  

It is a double privilege to be invited first to present a plenary address and then to respond to five distinguished and thoughtful discussants. They each agree that the topic is important, and in general they agree with my thoughts about it, though with several important elaborations, explanations, and amendments. My response will be highly selective and will focus on their additions and some possible points of difference among us.

Imre Szecsödy and David Tuckett write from the greatest geographic distance and emphasize the broadest context of my remarks. Szecsödy speaks of the purpose of case reports as “opening windows on the analytic process” and quotes with approval the IPA committee that warned that “unless we make some wise decisions, psychoanalysis will be dead—at least as a therapeutic approach.

417

[[RunningHead]]

Commentary

Lawrence Friedman

Lawrence Friedman

129B East 71st Street
New York, NY 10021
Fax: 212-734-3399

Lawrence Friedman  

October 30, 1999. I thank David Pincus for his precise and concise appreciation of my article, and for his lucid, supportive argument. His clinical observations of extreme realism and nominalism in cognitive styles is absolutely fascinating.

I am glad to have the opportunity to join Pincus in declaring our respect for Thomas Kuhn, whose rich contribution to the history of science is all but drowned out by the noisy and opportunistic fuss about paradigms. Pincus reminds us that Kuhn was not a science debunker. Kuhn (1977) described science as “repeatedly producing powerful new techniques of prediction and control” (p. 332). He seems to have felt that science progresses in some sense, but not by progressively perfecting a consistent project, and not by painting a more and more accurate picture of the world. But if not in those ways, then, how does science achieve its gains? Kuhn was frank enough to admit that he had no answer for that, but he assumed that it involved induction (pp. 32-333). I suppose he thought that the reliability of induction (which he accepted as a puzzling fact) is something that can be capitalized on in any number of random ways, and yet always more effectively as time goes by. Whether or not that is a satisfactory answer, it shows that Kuhn was not an epistemological skeptic.

I am intrigued by Pincus's elaboration of the unity of physical and social realms, and by his suggestion that perhaps “biology” should be extended “beyond our bodies, to the complex organizations and cultures we create.” That seems to me quite plausible. After all, they're all parts of one world, aren't they, existing together in space and time? Doesn't our view of the world reflect and affect our biology, and reflect and affect the physical world, the same way the giant termite mounds of Australia reflect their biology, alter their lives, and shape the landscape? Pincus's elegant prospectus of a common language whets our appetite for his further work.

[[RunningHead]]

Empirie Ohne Konzept? Einführung in Die Psychoanalytische Konzept-Forschung

Anna Ursula Dreher. Stuttgart: Verlag Internationale Psychoanalyse, 1998, 215 pp. $45.00

Leon Wurmser

Leon Wurmser

904 Crestwick Road
Towson, MD 21286
Fax: 410-828-6652

Leon Wurmser  

Current German psychoanalysis has to a remarkable extent dedicated itself to systematic empirical research in psychotherapy and psychoanalysis, orienting itself strongly toward the methodology of the natural sciences and using experimental techniques. Representatives of this trend see themselves in more or less sharp contrast to what in traditional classical psychoanalysis has been considered “research”: the careful study of individual cases, in reports that at times read like novels, as Freud put it, a method that seeks its justification in what Freud regarded as the natural, almost obligatory conjunction of treatment and research. These two research traditions are contrasted in this book with a third approach. Anna Ursula Dreher, a Frankfurt psychoanalyst who worked closely with the late Joseph Sandler and others at the Hampstead Clinic, proposes a program of psychoanalytic research into concepts, a systematic clarification of psychoanalytic concepts and their various uses. This, she assures us, can be conducted on an empirical basis, the Greek word empeiría meaning experience. In her view, such an exploration should not restrict itself to careful scrutiny of the literature, as important as this is. Rather, she has envisioned a broadening of the observational field to include the de facto use of concepts. In a systematic study, experienced practitioners are interviewed individually. This is followed by transcribed discussions of these interviews by selected groups of participants, including experts.

990

Interpreting Interpretation: The Limits of Hermeneutic Psychoanalysis

Elyn R. Saks. New Haven: Yale University Press, 1999, 263 pp., $35.00

Cecilio Paniagua

Cecilio Paniagua

Corazon de Maria 2
28002 Madrid
Spain

Cecilio Paniagua  

Elyn R. Saks is Professor of Law, Psychiatry, and the Behavioral Sciences at the University of Southern California Law School, and a Research Clinical Associate at the Los Angeles Psychoanalytic Institute. One characteristic of this author's first psychoanalytic book is the uneven quality of its chapters. Saks reviews succinctly the history of hermeneutics in philosophy and makes a fair and intelligent synthesis of the work of eight representative thinkers of hermeneutic psychoanalysis: George Klein, Roy Schafer, Herbert Fingarette, Robert Steele, Serge Viderman, Wolfgang Loch, Donald Spence, and Robert Sharpe. Saks quotes extensively from them, making the important point that “despite their similar vocabularies, the hermeneuts seemed to mean very different things” (p. vii). She eloquently explains how in this area, too, psychoanalysis has become a babel of tongues. In her discussion, the pars pro toto fallacy of many of these views is clearly exposed, as she shows how these authors often make general theories out of particular observations.

The author defines five views concerning the hermeneuts' positioning on the truth of analytic interpretations: the clinical psychoanalysis model, the story model, the alternative metaphysics model, the metaphor model, and the interpretations-as-literary-criticism model. The last chapters are dedicated to “the argument from patient rejection”; by this the author means the repudiation some modalities of hermeneutic psychoanalysis would face if only analysands knew their true implications. She critiques the claim that analytic interpretations are concerned only with meanings, not with facts, suspecting that “the hermeneutic view is a kind of backpedaling: the failure of psychoanalysis to live up to its scientific promise” (p. 208). Saks renders a good service to psychoanalytic theory in her conceptual delineation of these hermeneutic categories. She ascribes representative theoreticians to each model. George Klein, the early Schafer, and, to a lesser degree, Holt and Holzman are hermeneuts who would fit the clinical psychoanalysis model. That model says “psychoanalysis is hermeneutic in the sense that it is not a natural science concerned with uncovering the neurophysiological causes of behavior” (p. 82).

[[RunningHead]]

Unformulated Experience: From Dissociation to Imagination in Psychoanalysis

Donnel B. Stern. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 1997, xv + 293 pp., $45.00.

Margaret Crastnopol

Margaret Crastnopol

515 28th Avenue East
Seattle, WA 98112
Fax: 206-860-3774
E-mail: Margretc@u.washington.edu

Margaret Crastnopol   Associate Director, Northwest Center for Psychoanalysis, Seattle; Supervisor of Psychotherapy and faculty, William Alanson White Institute for Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy, New York; Clinical Instructor, Department of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences and Department of Psychology, University of Washington.

In this wide-ranging yet pithy book, unformulated experience is described as “mentation characterized by lack of clarity and differentiation” and as “the uninterpreted form of those raw materials of conscious, reflective experience that may eventually be assigned verbal interpretations and thereby brought into articulate from” (p. 37). Formulated understanding, then, is the product of a dialogic process embedded in the individual's relationship with another person, an idea, or a thing. Writing in densely textured but surprisingly accessible prose, Donnel Stern exhaustively develops this theme, including the place of dissociation and its opposite—imaginative understanding—in both the clinical encounter and life itself. Along the way the reader is offered introductory discussions of postmodernism, poststructuralism, constructivism, hermeneutics, linguistics, and their variants. These philosophical strands are deftly intertwined with seminal interpersonalist concepts such as embeddedness, autocentricity versus allocentricity, contextualization in the interpersonal field, the cognitively organizing role of the self-system, and parataxis as opposed to syntaxis. Effectively enacting his own ideas about the interpretive process, the author presents his arguments in socratic fashion. He poses questions, complicates them, fashions a response, and then interrogates his own answer. This dialectical mode invites a similar freedom of inquiry in the reader, which makes reading Stern's book an exercise in active learning and “meaning making.”

In the first section, Stern explains that postmodernists of his stripe (including constructivists and hermeneuticists) believe that experience is fundamentally “ambiguous” and that it becomes conscious and understood through the process of interpreting it from a given vantage point. By rendering our experience into language, we structure and organize it along certain interpretive lines. Different perspectives offer differing “partial truths,” some of which can hypothetically be judged “truer” than others—that is, they can meaningfully be deemed more coherent, comprehensive, or useful. This argument contrasts with the more radical poststructuralist view that language is merely a set of conventions (rather than a representation of reality) and that truth itself is entirely arbitrary.

Phyllis Tyson  

In 1950 Sylvia Brody began a pilot observational study to learn how maternal behavior influenced infant development. She wanted to make explicit the experiences of infants during the oral phase, and so clarify the complexities of maternal care. She discovered, and reported in the classic Patterns of Mothering (1956), that the way a mother conducted the feeding of her infant was the best single index of her general behavior and attitudes toward the infant, and of her responsiveness to the infant's needs and wishes. She believed that this was so because of the particular anxieties aroused in the mother by the feeding situation, and also, she speculated, because a mother's behavior during feeding reflected her freedom to gain gratification in caring for her infant while at the same time wrestling with her own inner anxieties. The feeding situation proved to be an especially rich source of study because it allowed for consideration of many collateral maternal activities, such as body contact, tempo, visual and verbal communication, and a variety of accessory behaviors. By examining the behavior of mother and infant and their mutual and ongoing responses to each other, Brody sought to elucidate the ways in which the mother's unconscious conflicts influenced how she recognized and how appropriately she satisfied the infant's needs and wishes.

In 1963 Brody began a much larger study, to determine if her findings would be significant in a larger and more diverse sample of mothers and infants. She also wanted to determine if the patterns she had distinguished statistically and clinically during the first six months would be stable throughout an infant's first year. This work eventually became a longitudinal study of 131 mother/infant dyads over the course of the first year of the children's development. In the original study, which gave rise to Anxiety and Ego Formation in Infancy (1970), statistically significant relations were found between types of maternal behavior and qualities of infant development and behavior. A natural attrition of subjects diminished the sample somewhat over the years, but follow-up studies at seven years (Mothers, Fathers, and Children: Explorations in the Formation of Character in the First Seven Years [1978])

Alan Sugarman  

Glen Gabbard introduced the panel with a selective overview of the contributions made by Fred Busch. Gabbard stressed the manner in which Busch has built on the earlier work of Paul Gray. In particular, Gray's addition of an educative element in analytic technique has been emphasized and expanded by Busch. This approach to analytic technique strives to teach the analysand a new type of thinking—one that involves the close observation of one's associations. Resistance, in particular, is monitored closely, with the analyst listening to and teaching the analysand to listen for moments in the analytic process when resistance is operating. Busch's approach to psychoanalysis is meant to find a way to help the patient's ego oscillate between being in analysis and observing the free associations that characterize the analytic process. The goal of analysis shifts in such an approach from changing one's core unconscious fantasies to gaining mastery over them by thinking about them. Resistance analysis becomes the heart of analytic technique and not just a prelude to the deep interpretation of unconscious fantasy. Self-analysis becomes a key criterion for termination, as patients demonstrate a mastery of the analytic way of thinking and apply it to their own free associations, particularly noticing unconscious defensive activity aimed at keeping thoughts out of awareness.

Gabbard noted that Busch has departed from Gray's thinking in important ways. For example, Gray considers the primary threat to the ego to be a superego injunction externalized onto the analyst. Such a view leads to resistance analysis being equated with superego analysis.

Panel held at the Spring Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, San Diego, May 17, 1997.

Panelists: Glen O. Gabbard (chair), Fred Busch (author), Robert Caper, David L. Raphling.

Early Light on a “Relatively Neglected” Topic

Robert Stolorow

Robert Stolorow

11726 San Vicente Boulevard
Suite 410
Los Angeles, CA 90049

Robert Stolorow  

January 21, 2000. It was disheartening for me to read the first sentence of Peter Buckley's interesting and thoughtful review essay, “Personal History and the Origins of Psychoanalytic Ideas” (JAPA 47/4). That sentence reads, “The influence of personal history, subjective experience, and inner conflict on the origins of psychoanalytic ideas has been a relatively neglected subject” (p. 1392). Buckley is apparently unaware that this “neglected subject” was the central focus

686

of the book Faces in a Cloud: Subjectivity in Personality Theory (Stolorow and Atwood 1979). Our studies of the personal, subjective origins of the metapsychological theories of Freud, Jung, Reich, and Rank, revised and updated in the book's second edition (Atwood and Stolorow 1993), formed the basis of what we came to call intersubjectivity theory. In words rather similar to Buckley's, we wrote the following more than two decades ago: “This book is concerned with the subjectivity of psychological knowledge and especially with the subjective origins of universalized metapsychological doctrines …. By systematically interpreting the theories as psychological products embedded in the context of life history and personal phenomenology, we hope to deepen our understanding of the limiting role of these subjective factors. We further intend to use the psychobiographical analyses as points of departure for a discussion of the possibility of a more general and inclusive framework” (Stolorow and Atwood 1979, p. 173).

[[RunningHead]]

The Listener: A Psychonalyst Examines His Life

Allen Wheelis. New York: W. W. Norton, 1999, 256 pp., $25.95

Glen Gabbard

Glen Gabbard

P. O. Box 829
Topeka, KS 66601-0829
Fax: 785-272-9577
E-Mail: Gabbargo@menninger.edu

Glen Gabbard  

Few psychoanalysts have succeeded as novelists. Even though many psychoanalytic case studies sound fictional, the writing of compelling fiction requires what Eugene O'Neill called “a touch of the poet.” Most analysts do not come by that gift naturally, nor do they acquire it through reading the psychoanalytic contributions that grace the pages of our journals. Over a period of five decades, Allen Wheelis has shown himself to be an exception. Quietly, consistently, and unpretentiously, he has crafted a series of novels, including The Scheme of Things and The Doctor of Desire, while also writing substantial nonfiction works that have had a wide-ranging influence on students, experienced clinicians, and the educated public. Now in his eighties, Wheelis has published a memoir. In his usual self-effacing manner, he describes it as “not yet a self-portrait, more a self-sketch.” In keeping with this description, it is a selective memoir, leaving out substantial portions of his life narrative, including any details of his own analysis. It is a work of nonfiction with the poetic style of a novel. As one might expect from an analyst, the book involves an internal narrative that is somewhat free-associational, weaving in and out of external events in the author's life. As one would also expect, based on Wheelis's previous work, it is unflinching, beautifully written, and deeply affecting.

The reader learns that the author grew up in an impoverished Southern family, his early years haunted by the decline and death of a consumptive father. There are heartrending scenes from this formative period. Young Wheelis learns to shoot sparrows under the tutelage of his demanding and authoritarian father. When his aim is less than perfect, he is met with stony silence. Instead of being allowed to play baseball with his peers, he is made to cut the grass with a straight razor, a task that leaves his hands bleeding and that takes the entire summer to complete.

Building Bridges: Negotiation of Paradox in Psychoanalysis

Stuart A. Pizer. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 1998, 248 pp., $39.95.

Donnel Stern

Donnel Stern

24 East 82nd Street
New York, NY 10028
E-mail: Dstern@psychoanalysis.net

Donnel Stern   Donnel B. Stern, Training Analyst, William Alanson White Institute for Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy; faculty, New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis.

In recent years there has been increasing acceptance in psychoanalysis, as in other disciplines, that meanings that appear to be opposed are often better understood as complementary. The psychology of “both/and” replaces the psychology of “either/or.” In psychoanalysis, this trend can be traced to Winnicott's argument for the far-ranging significance of paradox in psychic life, and for the corresponding centrality of what he called “play” and “potential space.”

But as important as Winnicott has become in American psychoanalysis—especially, perhaps, to analysts such as Stuart Pizer, who think of themselves as relational—he remains quintessentially British. Pizer refers to him, for example, as “that artful dodger of a psychoanalytic author, comfortably nestled in a British literary tradition that conveyed sense through nonsense” (p. 5). Which is to say that it is unlikely that Winnicott's dense and allusive writings would have been produced by an American psychoanalyst. Winnicott is nevertheless there at the heart of this otherwise very American book. In its pragmatism, precision, and directness it is as characteristic of the psychoanalysis of this country today (at least the relational/interpersonal strand of it) as Winnicott's work was of the Britain of his time. Pizer appreciates the allusiveness of language and the elusiveness of meaning; but where Winnicott responded to these phenomena with poetry, Pizer's response is to strive for clarity in a literate prose in which he nevertheless appreciates poetry (and quotes some).

[[RunningHead]]

Countertransference, Self-Examination, and Interpretation

Jean Roiphe

Jean Roiphe

40 East 83rd Street
New York, NY 10028
E-mail: Jroiphe@aol.com

Jean Roiphe  

Fred Pine introduced the panel with an excerpt from the essayist and memoirist Vivian Gornick, who speaks from the vantage point of the journalist about many of the issues familiar to us as analysts under the rubric of countertransference: the attempt to know the other through one's own reactions, the danger of losing that knowing through too close and identificatory a response to the other, and the need to refind the other through achieving some distance once again.

Pine noted that we have shifted conceptually from viewing countertransference as an interference to viewing it instead as a valuable source of knowledge and understanding about the analysand, a shift made earlier with regard to our views of transference and resistance. Pine asked the panelists to present clinical examples of their approach to self-examination of countertransference, and of how this may aid them in their understanding of the patient. He raised the questions of how we know when to rely on such aspects of our experience of the patient, how central this form of knowing is to our analytic technique, and how we maintain our integrity and stability as analysts while allowing ourselves to be open to this emotional reactivity within us.

Lawrence Epstein's contribution to the panel focused on the “bad-analyst” countertransference feelings that must be tolerated by the analyst in working with a particular sort of patient whose defenses he characterized as “implosive.” He spoke of a set of patients, “imprisoned in their empathy for others,” who tend to deny or be selectively inattentive to experiences of the analyst's negative impact.

Panel held at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December 19, 1997.

Panelists: Fred Pine (chair), Lawrence Epstein, Henry F. Smith, Elizabeth Bott Spillius. Discussant: Glen O. Gabbard.

Commentary

Alfred Margulies

Alfred Margulies

1493 Cambridge Street
Cambridge, MA 02139
Fax: 617-244-0915
E-mail: Alfred_margulies@hms.harvard.edu

Alfred Margulies  

In the pale light that each upon the other throws.

Wallace Stevens

Can we now perhaps find the place where

strangeness was present, the place where a person

succeeded in setting himself free?

—Paul Celan

The stakes. Groping for words and a new conceptual framework, Warren Poland goes out on a limb. To many, his language—words like “witnessing” and “otherness,” and names like Heidegger and Levinas—will seem unfamiliar, strange, even out of place and unnecessary to psychoanalytic practice. With the concept of witnessing, though, Poland is after implications that outstrip our usual discourse and loom large in the background of our meaning making, Moreover, the philosophical stakes are larger still because Poland's topic by its very nature outstrips all discourse, not only the psychoanalytic. That is to say, Poland is after the inexpressible—the unspeakable—itself.

While he is never explicit, the connotations of his title and his references are unavoidable: Felman and Laub's book probes Holocaust voices of testimony; Emmanuel Lévinas survived the camps; Heidegger embraced the Nazis but after the war remained resolutely silent; and the very phrase “to bear witness” now implicates that singular unspeakable trauma of our century. The Holocaust is a philosophical, ethical, and religious black hole that absorbs but does not illuminate: it is an event that cannot be explained by other events, that nachträglich, after the fact and ever afterward, changes and altogether confounds historical comprehension. Why then this subtext? What are the stakes? What connection can this literature of extraordinary, unspeakable trauma possibly have with our workaday clinical world? Just why did Poland search here?

Poland, I submit, is after meaning that cannot be reduced to other, antecedent meaning. This is to say, he is in search of understanding that cannot be settled, that at best spirals into new meaning that is still not enough. Trauma, then, serves as a template for inadequate and so unstable comprehension.

Winnicott as a clinician and theorist is an inspirational figure for all but the coldest hearts in our field. At times it is hard not to get swept away by his images. Being swept away, of course, involves the dangers of idealization, and Winnicott it seems to me was keenly aware of these dangers. He steadfastly avoided being engulfed by a great figure or theoretical position. 515 28th Avenue East

Margaret Crastnopol  

Relational Perspectives on the Body, a compilation of twelve papers edited by Lewis Aron and Frances Sommer Anderson, aims to enhance the clinician's capacity to foster an integration of patients' somatic experience with their cognitive and affective life. The contributors discuss the interrelated themes of the somatic representation of psychic issues; normal and pathological physical states; enactments; affective expression; somatic memories; and shifting states of consciousness.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

What Evil Means to Us

C. Fred Alford. Ithaca: Cornell University Press, 1997, 185 pp., $22.50

J. Meloy

J. Meloy

964 Fifth Avenue
Suite 409
San Diego, CA 92101
Fax: 619-544-1418

J. Meloy  

What does the cliche “Evil spelled backward is live” mean? Fred Alford wants to know, and with this provocative question he takes the reader on an intellectual journey which is, in his words, a “domestic anthropology” of evil.

Alford is an empiricist. He interviewed fifty “free informants” and spent a year conducting a weekly discussion group on evil with eighteen inmates at the maximum security prison in Patuxent, Maryland. His study sets this monograph apart from other works on evil, and will capture the attention of even the most jaded reader of polemics on the subject, such as myself.

The thesis of Alford's study is that the experience of evil is the experience of formless dread: the emotional knowledge that we are human, vulnerable, alone, and doomed to die. The doing of evil is the evacuation of this experience by intentionally inflicting it on others: a moment of fearless Faustian omnipotence for the evildoer.

Alford supports his thesis, and elegantly argues it, in a text that travels from Ogden's autistic-contiguous organization, through Klein's paranoid-schizoid position, to Kant's categorical imperative, with selected detours into the writings of Winnicott, Kierkegaard, Nietzsche, and Pagels, as well as my own work. He puts flesh to this scholarly bone by quoting the views of his informants—within prison and outside—and discussing contemporary popular icons of evil, such as vampires and several high-profile criminals in the United States. This is, I suspect, but a small sample of Alford's intellectual reach, which surprised me since he is a professor of government and politics, and to my knowledge is neither a psychologist nor a psychoanalyst.

1015

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Commentary

Warren Poland

Warren Poland

Warren Poland  

Having feared the dryness of scholastic contention, I find instead I have been fed a feast. It is a delight to have one's thoughts considered by so eminent a group of colleagues and to find those thoughts significantly extended by their remarks. I feel both appreciation and admiration.

Viewing the broad range of orientations among our discussants, I am struck by how central to their varied responses is the same issue that arose when these ideas were presented orally around the country. The dispute regularly stirred was which idea should take priority, the primacy of the individual or that of the group context out of which the individual takes shape. Which comes first?

Our discussants represent, and represent well, the opposing sides in this debate. Marcia Cavell, impatient with my view of intersubjectivity, properly points out that everyone is inevitably a unique individual. Emanuel Berman and Edgar Levenson, by contrast, seem impatient with my one-person view of the patient: they point out with equal correctness that everything occurring in an analysis comes to life and unfolds only within an interpersonal field. I trust that readers are not surprised to find me agreeing with both positions. Whatever my success, my aim has been to find a way to integrate the seemingly contradictory but essentially true premises from which the two sides start. To respond to this point at length would be to repeat my paper.

Regarding the specific commentaries, brevity demands that I touch lightly on major points in each while acknowledging the existence of more subtle implications meriting greater consideration.

Jorge Ahumada, like an artist stepping back from a partial image to place it within the broad context of an entire canvas, locates witnessing within the larger panorama of psychoanalytic history, specifically in what he succinctly terms the Berlin-Budapest debate. That dispute, which started with Freud and Ferenczi, is the historical setting in which we now work to resolve our seemingly irresolvable paradox. Always we face the powerful temptation, despite efforts to maintain balance, to conclude that both sides are right but that our personally favored side is more right. We act like the Englishman who picked up a piece of cutlery and said, “Funny. The French call this ‘un couteau’ the Germans call it ‘ein messer,’ and we call it ‘a knife’—which it is!

[[RunningHead]]

On Jonathan Lear

Marcia Cavell

Marcia Cavell

1570 Olympus Avenue
Berkeley, CA 94708
Fax: 510-548-5110
E-mail: Cavell@uclink4.berkeley.edu

Marcia Cavell   Visiting Associate Professor of Philosophy, University of California, Berkeley; candidate, San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute.

Open Minded: Working Out The Logic Of The Soul. By Jonathan Lear. Cambridge: Harvard University Press, 1998, 342 pp., $35.00.

In some of his most illuminating essays, Freud wrote as a social critic, observing broad cultural tendencies from a point of view that was simultaneously detached and compassionate, discerning of human frailty, and not very hopeful. A few other psychoanalysts have from time to time assumed this prophetic role—for example, Wilhelm Reich in his saner early years, Bruno Bettelheim, and Erik Erikson. But beyond the professional journals, most psychoanalysts these days are silent.

Jonathan Lear is an eloquent exception. In these essays he takes on the Freud-bashers, traditional philosophy, and psychoanalysis.1 “Every profession in its heyday—and psychoanalysis was no exception—tends to be seduced by it own wishful self-image and to make claims for itself that it cannot ultimately sustain,” inevitably inviting attack and revisionist criticism (p. 18). Freud himself, no one now doubts, made serious mistakes in both theory and practice. Philosophy perpetuates a myth of rationality. And the Freud-bashers, in giving the unconscious a brush-off, are making a mistake of oedipal dimensions. The “oedipal” tragedy in Sophocles' play was not that Oedipus married his mother and killed his father, Lear remarks in the essay titled “Knowingness and Abandonment: An Oedipus for Our Time.” It lay rather with his pretense to be a self-made man, his presumption that rational knowingness is all. Whether we call the unknown “Fate” or “the unconscious,” tragic consciousness in any culture consists in the recognition that human beings live their lives, as Lear puts, it, “in the intersection of two realms of meaning” (p. 53). This is what the Freud-bashers deny.

1The Journal of Philosophy, for which I also reviewed this book, has kindly given me permission to review it for this journal as well. There is little overlap, however, between the two reviews.283

Commentary

Jorge Ahumada

Jorge Ahumada

Av. Las Heras 3898 3H
1425 Buenos Aires
Argentina
Fax: +54-114-1-804-8110
E-mail: Jahumada@impsatl.com.ar

Jorge Ahumada  

The wiser a paper, the more difficult its challenges. Academic bickering finds scant place, and parallel lines of thought must earn their relevance.

Warren Poland's Toronto address is, I think, wise in keeping close to clinical facts and processes. Its clarity and remarkable freedom from theoretical jargon help to convey his views across conceptual chasms, as does its highlighting of a core analytic function, that of being witness to the analysand's ongoing psychic productions. What, then, can stand as comment? After addressing the patient's “testimony,” I shall delve, at my peril, into the topic of metapsychological frameworks and their consequences.

The patient's evolving “testimony” conveys and advances personal “truths” not consciously known. The term truths here, though, must be taken with a grain of salt: regarded as certainties, they may at second look reveal themselves as half-truths, if not outright misconceptions. Recourse can be made here to Bertrand Russell's distinction between “knowledge by acquaintance” (1911, 1948), which may be extended to include what the analysand unconsciously “knows” well enough to strenuously unknow it, and an “ostensive knowledge,” what can be pointed to and turned fully conscious. I trust that Poland agrees the analysand unknows core aspects of a “testimony” that at its most recondite he mainly enacts, and that such testimony entangles the analyst, by action and by the perlocutionary uses of language, its “emotive” and “actional” effects on the listener. The analyst, when on his or her game, can provide a conjectural version of what Russell calls “knowledge by description” of what is going on in the consulting room.

Freud taught us that this elicits resistance on both sides. The patient “knows” too much, has learned how to unknow it, and defends this unknowing; the patient's testimony protagonizes

[[RunningHead]]

Standing in the Spaces: Essays on Clinical Process, Trauma, and Dissociation

Philip Bromberg. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 1998, 376 pp., $55.00.

Philip Ringstrom

Philip Ringstrom

5004 Haskell Avenue
Encino, CA 91436
Fax: 818-906-3269
E-mail: Ringsite@aol.com

Philip Ringstrom  

From its inception, psychoanalytic literature has shown two broad styles of writing. In search of scientific validation, it has been rich in conceptualization that can at times be dauntingly pedantic. To capture the hearts and minds of psychoanalytic practitioners, and of Western civilization more generally, it has been equally rich in the evocative use of language and metaphor. This dual style was Freud's legacy. Having won the Goethe award for literature but none for science, he ultimately lamented: if you don't believe me, “Go ask the poets.”

In this gem of a book, Philip Bromberg straddles these two traditions in analytic writing, as evidenced in its title: Standing in the Spaces: Essays on Clinical Process, Trauma, and Dissociation. At first blush, one might ask, “Standing in what?” Then quickly comes the realization that one will be learning about trauma, dissociation, and how both influence clinical process. And indeed one will. There are between these covers nineteen essays spanning as many years, a period in which Bromberg's wisdom as a major contributor to the relational school of psychoanalysis has truly blossomed.

328

[[RunningHead]]

Seduction, Surrender, and Transformation

Karen J. Maroda. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 1999, viii + 207 pp., $39.95

Barbara Pizer

Barbara Pizer

152 Brattle Street
Cambridge, MA 02138
Fax: 617-354-0662

Barbara Pizer  

Describing the impetus for writing her second book, Karen Maroda remarked, at a Division 39 Meet the Author seminar last year, that “we've been talking about a two-person approach for a number of years now … but we have yet to really revolutionize psychoanalytic practice in a meaningful way that includes both people.”

Evocatively titled Seduction, Surrender, and Transformation, the book is centered on Maroda's ardent plea for emotional honesty in the analytic situation. In her unselfconscious, shoot-from-the-hip style, Maroda shares with us the intimate details of interactions with her patients. Augmenting the rich clinical material with research findings and other relevant literature on affect and cognition, she enters into a dialogue of agreement and disagreement with other eminent authors and clinicians, all of which is meant to demonstrate that positive therapeutic outcome depends on the emotional engagement of both partners in the analytic couple.

Maroda refers to herself as a relational analyst. She tells us her book is devoted to exploring the complexities that arise from taking the position that the two-person relationship serves as a vehicle for therapeutic action. She asserts that “the core understated aspects of this therapeutic action involve mutual emotional honesty, mutual emotional availability, and an atmosphere of mutual trust” (p. 38). In chapter 1 we learn from Maroda, through case example and collegial dialogue, what she means by mutuality. Whereas Freud attributed the power of seduction to women patients endangering their analyst's neutrality, Maroda stresses that psychological seduction is a mutual phenomenon, generic to the analytic situation. She goes on to challenge the theoretical underpinnings of our profession that have kept the person of the analyst in his or her head and out of the living relationship.

969

[[RunningHead]]

Internal Objects Revisited

Joseph Sandler and Anne-Marie Sandler. London: Karnac Books, 1998, 192 pp., $32.50 hardcover, $26.95 paperback

Howard Levine

Howard Levine

124 Dean Road
Brookline, MA 02146
Fax: 617-738-5011
E-Mail: Hblevine@aol.com

Howard Levine  

Joseph Sandler was a wise and productive contributor to psychoanalysis. In collaboration with his wife Anne-Marie and other colleagues, he laid the foundations for a theory of object relations and an associated clinical standpoint that enriched ego psychology and moved it toward a two-person perspective. His untimely and unexpected death, which occurred shortly after the publication of this book, left all who knew or learned from him saddened and impoverished. Included among the many works he left behind is this slender but powerful volume, which provides readers a thoughtful retrospective synthesis of his contributions to psychoanalytic theory and stands as a fitting memorial to his work.

At the heart of the Sandlers' contributions is the assumption that unconscious fantasies and their associated affects are structured around specific wishes for gratifying relationships between the self and significant objects. “Much of our life is involved in the concealed repetition of internalized early object relationships,” including “safety-giving or anxiety-reducing manoeuvres, as well as those that satisfy instinctual wishes” (p. 62). These wishes for relationship press inexorably toward actualization in the external world. “There is a constant need in every individual to externalize his or her ‘internal objects’ (the introjects), to anchor the inner world as far as possible in external reality” (pp. 19-20).

In an important extension of traditional classical theory, the Sandlers argue that conscious and unconscious feeling states, which they believe are the basis of psychological motivation, are determined by more than drive derivatives. In particular, they emphasize the significance to the individual of relationships that provide psychological “nourishment,” affirmation, and reassurance. These affective relational experiences serve a fundamental human need, as they preserve and restore feelings of safety and security. “In every situation of anxiety … there is a reaching towards the object that can provide safety, and this applies as much to the phantasy objects derived from introjects as to external objects” (p. 25).

[[RunningHead]]

Writing About Books

Musings and Suggestions from Two Editors

Salman Akhtar and Glen Gabbard

Glen Gabbard

1201 Chestnut Street, Suite 1503
Philadelphia, PA 19107
Fax: 215-568-2329
E-mail: Salman.Akhtar@mail.tju.edu

P.O. Box 829
Topeka, KS 66601-0829
Fax: 913-272-9577
E-mail: Gabbargo@menninger.edu

Glen Gabbard   Bessie Walker Callaway Distinguished Professor of Psychoanalysis at the Menninger Clinic, Training and Supervising Analyst at the Topeka Institute for Psychoanalysis, and Book Review Editor of Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association.

… the present book is decidedly obscure, composed in an artificial fashion and badly written.

—Freud, on the Ego and the Id (Jones 1957, P. 99)

While all books are written to be read, the fact is that some are and some are not. The authors of the former are the lucky ones. Even more fortunate are authors whose books become the subject of others' writing. At times, such a response is complex and lengthy enough to constitute a book itself. Kurt Eissler's Talent and Genius (1971), written as a rejoinder to Paul Roazen's Brother Animal (1969), a book about the Freud-Tausk relationship, is an example of this sort. Most published books evoke less magisterial reactions. Response to them is restricted to reviews in professional journals. Such reviews vary considerably in their scope, length, literary merit, and sophistication. These variations in the material written about books are our focus here.

With the goal of highlighting the broad range of commentary on psychoanalytic books, and in the hope that familiarity with this range might help potential book reviewers who are staring at their word processors awaiting the inspiration to transform their thoughts into words, we offer our observations. At the risk of sounding obsessionally taxonomic, we distinguish seven categories of material written about books: (i) dust jacket comment, (ii)
