Commentary

Sydney Pulver

Sydney Pulver

III North 49th Street
Philadelphia, PA 19139
Fax: 215-472-7718
E-mail: Spulver@bellatlantic.net

Sydney Pulver  

Analysts asked to give a plenary presentation to the American Psychoanalytic Association usually try to pick a topic they consider important to the field. Robert Michels addresses the case report, and it is hard to imagine how anything could be more important. He focuses on the very essence of the way we try to communicate with one another and with the rest of the world about what we do.

Michels makes two main points. First he describes the many problems associated with the case report since Freud, somewhat uncomfortably, began using it. That there are problems is evidenced by the relative scarcity of comprehensive case reports in our literature. Their place is taken almost always by brief clinical vignettes. Constraints of time and space, of course, account for some of this, but Michels delineates several other factors that compound the difficulty of preparing case reports: (1) the question of what a good case report should contain; (2) the difference between oral and written reports; (3) the issue of objectivity versus subjectivity; (4) the question of how data should be obtained; (5) the question of at what point case reports should be written; and (6) issues of confidentiality and patient consent.

But Michels's main point is that case reports are always written for specific purposes. Some of these the author is aware of; some he is not. Willy-nilly, the author's intentions influence both the form and the content of the report, often in very revealing ways. Michels urges us, if we wish to derive the fullest understanding of each report we encounter, to pay attention to the author's aims, both conscious and unconscious.

Content factors. The issue of just what a good case report should contain seems to be fairly straightforward. “The case history,” says Michels, “is to be a narrative of what happened, illustrated with vignettes rather than verbatim paragraphs, that pays attention to minute details and includes verbatim dialogue. It must account for the patient's symptoms, their meaning, and the patient's life history, as well as the analyst, the analytic process, the audience, and the analyst's intentions in writing it.” Discussant David Tuckett elaborates: “The account must … include information about how the analyst felt and how at the moment he or she understood both the patient's words and his or her own responses.

A test for Wallerstein's question—One psychoanalysis or many?—is to review several Lacanian books at once. Finding one's bearings in Lacan-land is difficult enough, but one quickly discovers even here a plurality of universes.

The fogs of geographical and cultural remoteness have allowed these lands to be thought of as unitary, but Lacan's longtime right-hand man, Jacques Perrier, in his Swiftian Voyages Extraordinaires en Translacanie (1985), could take for granted the multi-insularity of the “Translacanian Archipelago,” so named with a knowing wink to the Gulag. Perrier, however, continued to consider Lacan a genius, and in a way he was. His “return to Freud” recast psychoanalysis as a “psychoanalytic discourse” tailored to the idola fori

Martin Frommer   Supervising Analyst and Faculty, Institute for Contemporary Psychotherapy

As psychoanalysis enters its second century, its uneasy and often stormy relationship with homosexuality appears to be giving way to a rapprochement. I am tentative here because the current range of psychoanalytic views regarding homosexuality makes it difficult, as well as dangerous, to generalize about how the profession as a whole regards gay men and women at the millennium. This diversity reflects changes in cultural attitudes about homosexuality as well as a paradigm shift in the approach to general problems of epistemology and questions about what actually constitutes knowledge, evidence, truth, and fact. The result for psychoanalysis has been a gradual move away from a positivist position and to a new appreciation for the centrality of the analyst's subjectivity as the guiding force behind the meaning-making process in psychoanalytic theory and practice both. Nowhere is this more evident than in the ways in which gender and sexuality are being debated and reconfigured in contemporary psychoanalytic discourse.

Within the profession itself there are concrete indications of change where homosexuality is concerned. Lesbian and gay psychoanalysts have come out, and have been accepted into mainstream psychoanalytic institutes. Over the past decade their positions have been expressed more openly, and heard with greater interest. What can we learn about working with homosexual patients from perspectives that are both experience-near and analytically astute? If Jack Drescher's Psychoanalytic Therapy and the Gay Man illustrates this kind of perspective at work, then the answer is—a great deal.

The book's title does not indicate the breadth of its contents. This book is not only about psychoanalytically oriented psychotherapy with gay men. It is also a narrative of the relationship between psychoanalysis and the political struggle for gay rights.

1629

Cecile Bassen  

How do psychoanalysts think about humor, and about the use of humor in psychoanalysis? This multiauthored collection of essays explores both questions in considerable detail. Most of these papers are thought-provoking, many are well written, and some are delightful. Essays by ten authors (Martin Bergmann, Jean Sanville, Barnaby Barratt, James Grotstein, Peter Giovacchini, Ronald Baker, William Meissner, Judith Dupont, Robert Rodman, and Stuart Feder) are divided into three sections: historical and theoretical perspectives on the concept of humor; the role of humor in the therapeutic process; and the relationship of humor to character and creativity. There is rich material here for those who have neither read nor thought much about the meanings and uses of humor, both in and out of the therapeutic situation. Feder's chapter on the role of humor in the creation of a piece of music by Charles Ives is a surprise delight, which readers will appreciate regardless of any prior interest in Ives or his music.

The positive, life-enhancing aspects of humor are elaborated throughout this book. Bergmann describes humor as the product of a developmental stage made possible when the superego is able to adopt a benign and loving attitude toward the ego. He traces this line of thought back to Freud and describes its elaboration by Chasseguet-Smirgel, who wrote of humorists as trying to be their own loving mother, and by Warren Poland, who described the development of a sense of humor as a desirable outcome of analysis, involving an attitude of acceptance and laughter toward oneself and one's place in the world.

Sanville, Grostein, and Giovacchini all stress the association between humor, play, and the creation of a transitional space where creativity can blossom.

1005

Analytic Technique Today

Sander Abend

Sander Abend

245 East 87th Street # 17G
New York, NY 10128
Fax: 212-744-5751

Sander Abend  

When Freud first devised psychoanalysis, it was as a specialized medical technique for the treatment of certain kinds of neuro-psychological ailments, the neuroses. Although he never abandoned his convictions regarding the biological substrate of those conditions, experience soon led him to drop the effort to conceptualize either the illnesses or the treatment method in any terms other than purely psychological ones. He also came to appreciate that an individual need not be trained as a physician in order to understand the problems analysis was intended to relieve, or to practice the method successfully. As time went on, he and his followers applied his invention to a broader range of emotional problems, including what later generations of analysts would regard as conditions resulting from deviations in normal development, as well as some psychoses and borderline states.

Not only did Freud change his ideas about psychoanalysis as he went along, but, as is well known, fundamental differences of opinion about the technique soon arose among his former students. First to appear were arguments about content, that is to say, about the exact nature of the insight into the workings of their minds that analysands should be helped to acquire. Adler and Jung were merely the first in a long line of analysts whose views on that subject departed sharply from those of Freud, and today's pluralism is certainly marked by a multiplicity of views on what sorts of insight, if any, are crucial to the curative effect of psychoanalysis. It is probably safe to say that one thing analysts today all agree on, regardless of theoretical allegiance, is the importance of unconscious mental life. However, the varying emphasis they place on the attainment of insight into that life (as against other dimensions of the analytic experience) and, even more important, their differing opinions about what the vital content of such new self-knowledge should be, pose another question altogether.

No Self-Disclosure, Just a Typo

Henry Smith

17 Hammond Street

Henry Smith  

May 2, 2000. Sometimes a typographical error illuminates a theoretical point. In my recent article, “Countertransference, Conflictual Listening, and the Analytic Object Relationship” (JAPA 48/1, p. 109), the following should have appeared:

A patient says he is angry at his male lover. He is not there and should be. If I catch the patient's loneliness, I may speak to it. I know that feeling. I might say, “You feel so lost without him,” and I could be speaking to myself as well.

I am making the point here that I have not really “found” the patient but rather some mix of him and me. Because the analyst's picture of the patient is always a blend of the patient and the analyst, interpretations sometimes feel to the analyst as if they could be directed to both parties in the relationship.

It has been brought to my attention that because, in the published version, the closing quotation marks are inadvertently omitted from my comment to the patient, the sentence reads as if I might have said to the patient, “You feel so lost without him, and I could be speaking to myself as well”; in other words, the second clause becomes a self-disclosure, rather than a comment to the reader, as it is in the corrected version above. If this passage is read as a self-disclosure, it casts an entirely different light on the discussion that follows it, not one that I intended.

While the boundaries between patient and analyst are always blurred, whether or not one chooses to make this explicit to the patient remains a matter of personal choice. For myself, I would do so only under special circumstances. What I am trying to describe here is an effort to preserve the external boundary between analyst and patient in order precisely to allow the analyst's internal loss of boundaries to flourish and fuel the work.

I am grateful for the opportunity to clarify this point.

599

Working in Depth: A Clinician's Guide to Framework and Flexibility in the Analytic Relationship

Elliot Adler and Janet Lee Bachant Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson, 1998, 294 pp., $60.00

Kimberlyn Leary

Kimberlyn Leary

527 East Liberty Street
Suite 209 D
Ann Arbor, MI 48104

E-mail: Kimleary@umich.edu

Kimberlyn Leary   Associate Director, University of Michigan Psychological Clinic; candidate, Michigan Psychoanalytic Institute

Elliot Adler and Janet Lee Bachant's Working in Depth: A Clinician's Guide to Framework and Flexibility in the Analytic Relationship is a welcome addition to the literature on the teaching and learning of psychoanalysis. The authors have produced a thoughtful—at times, luminescent—reflection on analytic process and the working life of the clinical analyst. The book's nine chapters (Getting Started; Free Association and Analytic Neutrality; Dimensions of Transference; Intrapsychic and Interactive Dimensions of Resistance; Analysis of Countertransference; Hearing the Unconscious; Interpretive Activity; Therapeutic Action; and The Analyst's Growth and Maturation) follow the trajectory of an analysis from its inception into the middle phase. Along the way, Adler and Bachant take up topics ranging from the pain accompanying real change (chapter 4) to the technical requirements of analytic listening (chapter 6) and the way that description and acknowledgment may function as clinical interventions (chapter 7).

The result is an effective teaching text, richly evocative of the challenges, tensions, and pleasures of learning to engage deeply with patients in ways that promote growth and development in both patient and analyst. The clinical vignettes present an array of ordinary treatment situations (e.g., threats to the treatment frame, intense transferences, and the analyst's narcissistic investments) in a way that is attentive to the anxiety that they may generate in young clinicians. The final chapter includes a particularly moving meditation on the emotional demands analytic practice places on analysts, their families, and their friendships. Overall, the book is well written, clearly conceptualized, and oriented to the pragmatics of clinical practice, making it suitable for analytic candidates as well as for medical residents and interns in clinical psychology and social work.

615

Transcending the Self: An Object Relations Model of Psychoanalytic Therapy

Frank L. Summers. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 1999, 280 pp., $45.00

Ronald Baker

Ronald Baker

12 Southwood Lawn Road
Highgate

England

Ronald Baker   Training and Supervising Analyst, British Psychoanalytical Society; member, Royal College of Psychiatrists

Frank Summers's emphasis in this book is on the clinical process. A detailed clinical example serves as an introduction to a focus that recurs throughout the volume—namely, a comparing and contrasting of the ego psychological approach, an object relations approach based largely on Winnicott's contributions, and the contemporary relational viewpoint.

From the ego psychological position, symptoms are seen as a neurotic compromise between drive-induced wishes and superego prohibition. The analytic task is to make the unconscious conscious, with the ego achieving greater mastery over unconscious derivatives and reaching more adaptive compromises. This allows for a greater degree of instinctual gratification. This is a one-person model, with inquiry focused entirely within the patient.

For relationalists, symptoms are compromises reflecting conflictual relational patterns between patient and analyst. Analyst and patient cocreate the clinical material, and both are inextricably embedded in the relational matrix. The goal is to broaden and enrich the patient's relational configuration. Therapeutic action lies primarily in this new relationship created by patient and analyst. Relationalists criticize ego psychology and other approaches as too narrowly focused on intrapsychic life and as undervaluing the inherently interactive nature of the self.

Summers's object relations theory views primary symptoms as rooted in early relationships. Object relations theorists seek to explore, understand, and create a new self-structure rather than facilitate a more enriching relationship. They aim to help the patient reject destructive patterns and replace them with patterns based on authentic experiences.

622

[[RunningHead]]

Reverie and Interpretation: Sensing Something Human

Thomas H. Ogden. Jason Aronson, 1999, 286 pp., $30.00 paperback

James Grotstein

James Grotstein

522 Dalehurst Avenue
Los Angeles, CA 90024
Fax: 310–474–8075
E-Mail: Jgrot3456@cs.com

James Grotstein   Training and Supervising Analyst at the Los Angeles Psychoanalytic Society/Institute and Psychoanalytic Center of California, Los Angeles; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, UCLA School of Medicine

In his most remarkable work to date, Reverie and Interpretation: Sensing Something Human, Thomas Ogden quotes Debussy as saying, “Music is the space between the notes.” This work can be likened to a symphony between the thematics of Winnicott, Klein, Bion, Frost, and Ogden. One at first wonders why a poet, Robert Frost, is listed with these analysts. Why he is included is perhaps the centerpiece of this highly innovative and creative work. The Ariadne's thread that runs through Ogden's book is aliveness and humanness, which he seems to proffer for candidacy as supraordinate measures of the success of a psychoanalytic experience. He makes use of poetry, fiction, and literary criticism, in addition to a large body of psychoanalytic literature, to explore the use of language in psychoanalysis. He is particularly interested in the way we succeed or fail at bringing language to life, and life to language, in the analytic dialogue.

Words, written or spoken, are to be met halfway by the reader or listener, who must actively and imaginatively participate in this intersubjective venture. Analysts, as well as analysands, must be encouraged to become “makers of music rather than players of notes.” In a chapter titled “Listening: Three Frost Poems,” Ogden presents us with Frost's advice to read aloud so as to get the feel of the words in our mouths—i.e., the aliveness that resides within the sounds and other physical experiences involved in “saying the lines.”

I found this work to be an eloquent invitation into a new phase of development for psychoanalysis. Though solidly based on classical, Kleinian, neo-Kleinian, Bionian, and Winnicottian foundations, it is nonetheless innovative, daunting, and provocative. The book's title is Reverie and Interpretation, but it is the subtitle, Sensing Something Human, that captures the simple essence of this work, which prefigures a radical change in how we view psychoanalytic theory and technique.

Ogden's contributions are hard to categorize. His is a unique American voice profoundly steeped in the works of Winnicott, Tustin, Meltzer, Klein, and Boyer, among others, but he always manages to give a unique interpretation of and extension to their works. In his conception of the autistic-contiguous position (the focus of his The Primitive Edge of Experience)

Learning Disabilities and Psychic Conflict: A Psychoanalytic Casebook

Arden Aibel Rothstein and Jules Glenn. Madison, CT: International Universities Press, 1999, viii + 504 pp., $78.00.

Ernest Kafka

Ernest Kafka

23 East 92 Street
New York, NY 10128
E-mail: Erkafka@pipeline.com

Ernest Kafka   Training Analyst, New York Psychoanalytic Institute

Rothstein and Glenn's Learning Disabilities and Psychic Conflict: A Psychoanalytic Casebook is a remarkable book; it is full of ideas and illustrative materials that are clearly presented, interestingly written, and highly worthwhile. Anyone interested in analysis, development, brain science—or any aspect of psychology, teaching, or mental health—will find profit and enjoyment in reading this book.

Eight analysts contribute nine case reports, mostly of thirty pages or so, of analytic work with people with learning disabilities. Five of the nine patients were children, one was an adolescent, and three were adults. The authors provide a discussion of about sixteen pages after each report. They summarize the cases, evaluate the available diagnostic testing as to findings and completeness, comment on any technical problems that arose in the work, and discuss the inter-relationships they perceive among definable, neurophysiologically based mental disabilities (as reflected in the psychological tests), developmental experiences, and transference and countertransference manifestations.

The authors show that specific limiting neurophysiologic givens (such as deficits in memory, impulse control, logical sequencing, the capacity to understand spatial relationships, and so on) influence development, and that psychological testing today can, to some extent, identify and quantify them.

Emanuel Berman  

I found Warren Poland's address quite evocative, often moving, and intriguing on several levels.

First, it can be contemplated in the context of Poland's own theoretical evolution, as a distillation of themes identifiable as his unique “handwriting,” as a further development of some of his lifelong concerns. In an early paper on tact Poland (1975) already speaks of achieving “a union of touching while acknowledging essential separateness” (p. 158). Later on he suggests that neutrality is “born out of [the analyst's] respect for the essential otherness of the patient” (Poland 1984, p. 283).

Anna and Sigmund at the Rue Royale

Eugene Mahon

Eugene Mahon

Eugene Mahon
8 East 96th Street
New York, NY 10128
Fax: 212-289-1950

Eugene Mahon  

In my imagination the history of ideas is a dialogue: the seminal thoughts of the past generating new meanings, new challenges, new contradictions for current listeners who are not passive receptacles of, but active communicators with, the great legacy of the past. This is an old intellectual notion, not a new one. What is the oedipus complex after all from an aesthetic point of view but a tension between one generation and another that turns the wheels of intellectual progress, even as it preserves them? The dialogue as scientific catalyst is as old as Plato, as provocative as Galileo, and as modern as Stanley Leavy who has suggested that psychoanalysis itself can be thought of as an enduring dialogue. In that time-honored intellectual tradition and spirit I invoke a posthumous dialogue between Anna and Sigmund Freud at a restaurant called the Rue Royale. It is a dialogue between the past and the present, and the coordinates are the royal road of intellectual discourse where legacy meets scientific truth with one interest only: the pursuit of meaning through the never-ending psychoanalytic investigation of the mind.

October 9, 1982. A table in a restaurant. An old man seated. A Beethoven piano sonata is heard softly in the background. A woman enters, old but youthful. (It will become clear, as the dialogue proceeds, that this is a meeting in the afterlife of Sigmund Freud and Anna Freud).

SIGMUND: I've waited since 1939 for this moment.

ANNA: Forty-three years! Nothing, in the larger scheme of things. A mere turn or two of the hourglass in tectonic time.

SIGMUND:

Training and Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Center for Training and Research, Columbia College of Physicians and Surgeons.

Submitted for publication April 18, 2000.

Howard Welsh

Howard Welsh

27 West 86th Street, Suite C

Howard Welsh   Training and Supervising Analyst, NYU Psychoanalytic Institute; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, NYU Medical Center.

In Rethinking Clinical Technique, Fred Busch continues to explicate Paul Gray's close process technique while at the same time differentiating aspects of his own work from that of Gray. Both Gray and Busch have emphasized the “developmental lag” in clinical technique since the theoretical basis for an ego psychology was heralded by Freud's “The Ego and the Id” and “Inhibitions, Symptoms and Anxiety.”

Although ego psychology brings to the forefront the existence of unconscious defenses and the necessity for their interpretation before addressing drive content, Gray and his disciples have made a convincing argument that the “new” ego psychology has not yet been fully integrated into clinical technique.

[[RunningHead]]

Subjectivity and Objectivity in the Analyst

Melvin Berg

Melvin Berg

Box 829
Topeka, KS 66601
Fax: 913-273-5522

Melvin Berg  

Glen Gabbard set the stage by reviewing the increasingly pluralistic nature of psychoanalysis. This pluralism is fostered by the postmodern emphasis on the interrelated perspectives of social constructivism, mutuality, intersubjectivity, relativism, and perspectivism. In contrast to Freud's positivist approach, these perspectives suggest that reality is a social construction created and negotiated between people. Whereas Freud's positivist orientation generated a dispassionate search for an objective truth, the postmodern position is that the inevitable influence of the analyst's subjective perceptions makes absolute objectivity impossible. An extreme position, relativism, holds that truth is impossible. The more moderate views of realism and perspectivism, held by many contemporary psychoanalysts, suggest that a workable understanding of the patient's reality emerges from the negotiated meaning between the perspectives of both participants in the analytic dialogue.

The clinical application of these notions leads to a focus on the intersubjective nature of the analytic process as delineated by Ogden, Natterson, Benjamin, Stolorow, and Gill. A radical application of these ideas was formulated by Owen Renik, who suggested that the intersubjective nature of psychoanalysis undermines the principles of neutrality and anonymity. Other intersubjectivists support the open expression of feelings and judgments by the analyst. While many support the current view of psychoanalysis as a two-person psychology, others such as Boesky warn of the potential dangers in forsaking neutrality, abstinence, and other aspects of standard technique.

Panel held at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December 21, 1996.

Panelists: Glen O. Gabbard (chair and discussant), Fred Busch, Darlene Bregman Ehrenberg, Owen Renik, Henry Smith.

539

[[RunningHead]]

Jacques Lacan

Elizabeth Roudinesco.; translated by Barbara Bray. New York: Columbia University Press, 1997, 496 pp., $39.00 hardcover, $19.50 paperback

William Richardson

William Richardson

Chestnut Hill, MA 02167
Fax: 617–552–2977

William Richardson   Professor of Philosophy, Boston College; graduate, William Alanson White Institute

Was he a genius? A charlatan? Was he mad? This lively account of the life of Jacques Lacan, the so-called French Freud, by Elizabeth Roudinesco, Parisian journalist and historian of psychoanalysis, provides reason, at one time or another, to think of him as each of these—and more.

Arguably the most creative speculative mind the psychoanalytic movement has produced since Freud himself, Lacan described his project as a return to the fundamental insight of Freud, namely, that the unconscious is structured like a language, as this may be discerned in Freud's decisive early works: The Interpretation of Dreams, The Psychopathology of Everyday Life, and Jokes and Their Relation to the Unconscious. This much is straightforward enough, but the enterprise unfolded in so serendipitous a fashion and was expressed in so baroque a style that Anglo-Saxon readers, at least in the psychoanalytic (as opposed to the literary) community, have been slow to accord Lacan's work the recognition it may well deserve. Recent secondary literature has ameliorated the situation, but great difficulty remains for the uninitiated to gain a sense (however incomplete) of the enterprise as a whole. What unity is to be found in it is perhaps to be discovered more easily in the bizarre coherence of Lacan's own colorful life, and this is what Roudinesco provides.

Jacques Emile Lacan (1901–1981), firstborn son of Albert Lacan and Emilie Baudry, came from petit bourgeois stock: the scion of successful vinegar merchants on his father's side and of a long line of conventional Roman Catholics on the mother's. Memories of his childhood were deeply unhappy, scarred by the odious influence of the paternal grandfather, Emile, hatred of whom taught the young Jacques (who bore Emile as his middle name) how to perform the essential act of cursing God. Apparently, the grandfather tyrannized the father, who became so intimidated that he allowed himself to be dominated by the mother as well.

Depth-Psychological Understanding: The Methodologic Grounding of Clinical Interpretations

Philip F.D. Rubovits-Seitz. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 1998, 496 pp., $69.95

Eric Gillett

Eric Gillett

25 Cypress Avenue
Kentfield, CA 94904
E-mail: Egillett@aya.yale.edu

Eric Gillett  

Brilliantly synthesizing a variety of ideas relevant to describing and justifying the interpretive process in psychoanalysis, Philip Rubovits-Seitz has written a book that should be of great interest to both clinicians and researchers. He begins with historical background underscoring Freud's relative neglect of the methodology of interpretation and its problems, a fact noted by Glover and others. Of major importance is the recent paradigm shift in general science from a positivist to a postpositivist perspective, the latter a stance that supports the compatibility of the interpretive approach with modern science. Later chapters deal with post-Freudian models comparing methods of interpretation in clinical and nonclinical fields. Rubovits-Seitz then turns, in a chapter that provides balanced treatment of Grünbaum's critique of psychoanalysis, to the problem of justifying interpretations. One of his most important contributions is an elaboration of what he calls the method of “recurrent cycles” first proposed by Thomas French.

“French discovered that the therapeutic process follows repetitive dynamic cycles that become steadily shorter (that is, the cycles are completed more rapidly) as the treatment progresses—a form of organized interlocking microstructure that the writer uses in conjunction with other validating methods to justify interpretations and reconstructions” (p. 249). Rubovits-Seitz notes that many cases do not reveal such a neat pattern of cycles, and it is a matter for future empirical research whether such cycles can be reliably identified. The method involves examining a process notes record of a completed therapy and, starting from the end, attempting to identify a cycle (a sequence of steps) that can be identified as repeated throughout the course of the treatment.

993

The Modern Freudians: Contemporary Psychoanalytic Technique

Carolyn S. Ellman, Stanley Grand, Mark Silvan, and Steven J. Ellman. Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson, 1998, 298 pp., $50.00.

Peggy Hutson

Peggy Hutson

3170 Munroe Drive
Miami, FL 33133
Fax: 305-446-7565
E-mail: Pjbhutson@aol.com

Peggy Hutson   Training and Supervising Analyst, Florida Psychoanalytic Institute; Clinical Professor, Department of Psychiatry, University of Miami School of Medicine.

In these times of competing psychoanalytic perspectives and significant change, the task of retaining what is valid, of creating solid new ideas, and of integrating them while avoiding power struggles is a great challenge. At a 1996 seminar in New York, this challenge was met successfully by an outstanding group of Freudian psychoanalysts from the NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis. Their papers, and an additional invited contribution, have found a home in The Modern Freudians: Contemporary Psychoanalytic Technique. This book, an admirable attempt to provide a framework for retaining the old while understanding newer analytic paradigms, is a model of communication and integration in this period of psychoanalytic transition. Difficulties inherent in the goals of the book and in psychoanalysts' efforts at communication come to life in the contributions.

The authors bring to the reader an excellent picture of what is Freudian about contemporary psychoanalysis. They spell out which Freudian principles have endured as fundamental in our field and why they have retained their relevance. Along the way, ideas that have been discarded or modified are addressed. The authors gathered here reflect the diversity in thinking among analysts currently working in the Freudian tradition. While most of their papers are clear, well researched, and respectful of the ideas of others, a few unfortunately fall short of this standard.

Two of the strongest contributions are found at the book's beginning and at its end. Arnold D. Richards and Arthur Lynch walk the reader through a quick but clear historical overview from ego psychology to contemporary conflict theory.

297

[[RunningHead]]

Tradition and Change in Psychoanalysis

Roy Schafer. Madison, CT: International Universities Press, 1997, xvi + 271 pp., $42.50

Jacob Jacobson

Jacob Jacobson

1636 16th Street
Boulder, CO 80302
Fax: 303-440-9222
E-mail: Jgj 1234@aol.com

Jacob Jacobson   Training and Supervising Analyst, Denver Institute for Psychoanalysis; Clinical Professor, Department of Psychiatry, University of Colorado Health Sciences Center.

Roy Schafer's latest book is a collection of papers previously published elsewhere, most in the 1990s. Continuity is provided by the author's search to understand the dialectic noted in the title. He endeavors to free himself—and us—from undue confinement by tradition, while yet remaining respectful and cognizant of its riches, and to differentiate changes that further the development of basic psychoanalytic principles from changes that evidence their abandonment.

In essays on Freud, Hartmann, and Eissler, Schafer sounds a note of profound respect for their achievements, while at the same time taking issue with them quite cheerfully where he believes we are now in a position to do better. Refreshingly, he does his best to deal with them in terms of the analytic and societal climate within which each was working.

311

[[RunningHead]]

What do Psychoanalysts Want? The Problem of Aims in Psychoanalytic Therapy

Joseph Sandler and Anna Ursula Dreher. New York: Routledge, 1996, 160 pp., $59.95 hardcover, $22.99 paperback

Robert Galatzer-Levy

Robert Galatzer-Levy

122 South Michigan Avenue
Chicago, IL 60603
Fax: 312–922–5084
E-mail: Gala@midway.uchicago.edu

Robert Galatzer-Levy   Training, Supervising, and Child and Adolescent Supervising Analyst, Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis; Lecturer in Psychiatry, University of Chicago

Very good ideas often have the peculiar quality of seeming obvious once they are understood, so much so that we must remind ourselves that we did not know them all along. The notion that studying clinical psychoanalysis requires attention to the analyst's aims is just such an idea. In clinical reports, systematic investigations, and even supervision, analysts' intentions are almost always left implicit, as if we all agreed about analytic goals and all that needs discussion is how to achieve them. Once the matter is stated, it becomes clear this cannot be right. The values and theories that inform each analyst's vision of a “good” analysis, the analyst's vision of the aims of analysis, must shape how that analyst proceeds and the extent to which the result is viewed as satisfactory.

Joseph Sandler and Anna Ursula Dreher provide a clear and nonpolemical guide to the shifting goals of analysts. They show how these shifts relate to other changes in psychoanalysis and how these changing goals are reflected in analytic technique. These clarifications invite sharper thinking about analytic goals, confrontation with the impact of our personal goals on analytic work, and greater appreciation of earlier contributions that out of context can be difficult to comprehend. Sandler and Dreher are particularly skillful at appreciating competing viewpoints on their own terms, a notable accomplishment when spanning the wide range of clinical psychoanalysis. Only once or twice in the entire volume does it seem likely that an author whose ideas are presented might have reason to cry foul.

One may invoke some version of neutrality to support the notion that analysts should aim only to “analyze.

649

Per Vaglum  

Both for psychiatric patients and for the psychoanalytic profession, it is very important that psychoanalytic knowledge and clinical experience continue to influence psychiatry and psychotherapy. An important source of such influence can be books that connect observable clinical phenomena with a meaningful synthesis of contemporary psychoanalytic theory and technical knowledge. Such books constitute a bridge between psychoanalysis and general psychiatric and clinical psychological practice, and can thereby positively influence recruitment to psychoanalytic training.

Countertransference Enactments Informed by Cancer in an Analyst's Child

Sandra Bemesderfer

Sandra Bemesderfer

268 Arlington Avenue
Kensington, CA 94707
Fax: 510-526-0905
E-mail: Sbemesder@aol.com

Sandra Bemesderfer   Training and Supervising Analyst, San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute. Submitted for publication September 1, 1998.

The author's son was diagnosed with Hodgkin's lymphoma in the spring of 1997. She traces the impact of this trauma on her practice, with specific emphasis on her experience as both analyst and mother, and on the related countertransference feelings and enactments. The paper discusses in detail the analyst's effort to determine for each patient whether or not to disclose the fact of her son's illness, the internal conflict she experienced over those decisions, and the effects of her decisions on her patients. An extensive case discussion focuses on a patient who appeared to intuit in a dramatic fashion the traumatic events in the analyst's private life.

responseSet.title: American Psychoanalysis in the New Millennium
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

American Psychoanalysis in the New Millennium

Glen Gabbard

Glen Gabbard

Glen Gabbard  

In the not too distant past, terms such as “American psychoanalysis,” “British psychoanalysis,” and “French psychoanalysis” had reasonably clear referents. When one spoke of the “American” perspective on psychoanalytic ideas, classical notions of intrapsychic conflict, compromise formation, the tripartite structural theory, and defense analysis immediately jumped to mind. The state of affairs as we enter the second century of psychoanalysis is not nearly as clear-cut. There is no longer a “party line” in American psychoanalysis, and there is little question that intellectual discourse has been greatly enhanced by the arrival on the American scene of pluralism in psychoanalytic theory.

The origins of this diversity can be traced to a number of influences. The interpersonalists, who have been developing their thinking over a period of several decades now, largely outside the mainstream of American psychoanalysis, are certainly one factor. The movement toward psychoanalytic work with seriously disturbed patients is another. The so-called widening scope was instrumental in introducing the ideas of Melanie Klein and British Independents such as Winnicott to American clinicians. I can vividly remember Jock Sutherland during his regular visits to the Menninger Clinic in the 1970s and 1980s consulting on seriously disturbed patients and observing, “You have to enter darkest ‘Melanie-sia’ to treat this patient.” Where once Kleinian ideas were downright “un-American,” we now witness marvelous teachers from the other side of the Atlantic making regular trips to our professional meetings and institutes to teach American analysts contemporary views of Kleinian theory and technique.

Similarly, in the context of our frustration with the treatment of narcissistic patients, Kohut's theory and technique emerged and was elaborated into self psychology.

293

The Consultation Process with an Adult

A Child Analyst's Perspective

Claudia Lament

Claudia Lament

31 Washington Square West, PHA
New York, NY 10011
E-mail: Clament@dellnet.com

Claudia Lament   Faculty, New York University Psychoanalytic Institute; graduate in child psychoanalysis, Anna Freud Centre.

A close examination of a consultation is presented. Such a face-to-face encounter with an adult patient can suggest a parallel with another type of treatment: child analysis. It is argued that certain ways of working with children in analysis—the gliding between the interpersonal and the intrapsychic—were especially useful in bringing this adult patient to engage in analysis. Also highlighted in this account are interventions that function not as interpretations per se but as protointerpretations aimed at arousing preconscious affect states for the purpose of bringing them into conscious experience.

[[RunningHead]]

Being of Two Minds: The Vertical Split in Psychoanalysis

Arnold Goldberg. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 1999, 185 pp., $34.50

Leonard Horwitz

Leonard Horwitz

Box 829
Topeka KS 66601
Fax: 785–272–9577
E-mail: Horwitl@meninnger.edu

Leonard Horwitz   Training and Supervising Analyst, Topeka Institute for Psychoanalysis

The title of this book suggests a state of ambivalence or indecisiveness in which a person is unable to decide between alternative courses of action equally attractive or distasteful. However, this everyday sense of the phrase is hardly Arnold Goldberg's topic in this brief but provocative book. Rather, he is describing a form of pathology in which two divergent states of mind, two self-organizations each with its own values and goals, exist side by side, one predominating over the other at any given time.

The work is an elaboration and extension of Kohut's concept of the vertical split. Kohut originally introduced the concept to deal with the oscillation between feelings of grandiosity and inferiority that is often observed in narcissistic personality disorders. What he formulated was a split between a better structured, more adaptive self and a fragmented, more pathological one. The two facets of the vertical split have more or less equal access to consciousness, and one is kept at bay by the other by the mechanism of disavowal. By contrast, the horizontal split uses the classical topographic model, in which unacceptable impulses and defenses are kept out of consciousness by the mechanism of repression.

Goldberg amplifies these ideas along a continuum from normal to pathological, amply illustrated by richly described case material. He uses Kohut's distinction between narcissistic behavior disorder and narcissistic personality disorder, but most of the cases he presents involve lapses and eruptions into behavioral realms. The prototypical vertical split, he argues, is seen in marital infidelity. While the several marital problems he presents differ in certain details, they all involve men who are unable to tolerate or find sufficient gratification in a monogamous relationship. One of the cases is reminiscent of the film Captain's Paradise, in which a man feels equally devoted to his wife and his mistress and wants to give up neither: “Dirk was of two minds, but this was not a case of one hidden and one overt. Rather, they made alternating claims for control and dominance” (p. 106).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Influence of the Gender of Patient and Analyst in the Psychoanalytic Relationship

Otto Kernberg

Otto Kernberg

21 Bloomingdale Road
White Plains, NY 10605
Fax: 914-997-5997
E-mail: Okernber@mail.med.cornell.edu

Otto Kernberg   Director, Personality Disorders Institute, New York Hospital–Cornell Center, Westchester Division; Professor of Psychiatry, Cornell University Medical College; Training and Supervising Analyst, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

An overview of current explorations of the influence of gender on the psychoanalytic situation is presented. The topic does not lend itself to simple generalizations because the accumulated experience of several generations of psychoanalysts is now merging with newer views of psychoanalytic technique, while at the same time psychoanalytic theory is being influenced by changing ideological crosscurrents and by new knowledge regarding the similarities and differences in the development of both genders. Major issues still open are the relationship between gender and sexuality and between erotic desire and love; the challenges of the boundary of the psychoanalytic relationship as a facilitrating and containing frame for the exploration of oedipal conflicts; and the related temptations, prohibitions, and derivatives of the erotic tension in the transference-countertransference.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Why We Need to Write Openly About Our Clinical Cases

Stanley Coen

Stanley Coen

390 West End Avenue, IH
New York, NY 10024-6200
Fax: 212-362-0682
E-mail: Sjcoenmd@aol.com

Stanley Coen  

To learn more about what is and is not effective in the psychoanalytic situation, analysts need to develop a library of detailed analytic case material. Open case writing, by not overconstraining readers, allows them enough access to the analytic couple's affective experience to persuade them of the authenticity and validity of the analyst-author's views. They are then free to rethink the material creatively on their own, to use it as they need to, and to learn from others, in order to enhance their analytic skills. Closed case writing hinders readers' creative freedom to understand and interpret, and hence to learn. To encourage analyst-authors to share their affective experiences in the analytic situation more openly, analysts need to establish a constructively collaborative attitude toward playing with clinical material. They need to take for granted that new readers, even authors themselves newly rereading their work, will discover something new. A dialectical process can then be established in which clinical authors and readers contribute to each other in an ongoing imaginative interchange.

A Mind of One's Own: A Kleinian View of Self and Object

Robert Caper. London: Routledge, 1998, 176 pp., $85.00 hardcover, $29.99 paperback

John Steiner

John Steiner

28 Park Drive
London, NWII 7SP
England
Fax: +44 208–731–8193
E-mail: SteinerJ28@cs.com

John Steiner   Training Analyst, British Psychoanalytical Society

To have a mind of one's own, to be able to think for oneself and, as the author puts it, to know what one is and what one is not, is a fundamental requisite for mental health. That's the message of this important new book from Robert Caper. I imagine that few contemporary analysts will disagree and that many will be struck, as I was, by the freshness and originality of Caper's formulations. Moreover, it is implicit in his reasoning that a mind of one's own is essential for personality growth, the fulfillment of individual potential, and the development of appropriate relationships with others. Of course, the immediate question such a formulation raises is what it means not to have a mind of one's own, and it is one of the strengths of this book that its author provides a detailed discussion of the various ways the mind can be dismantled, modified, or disowned. This involves a recognition that the mind can be fragmented, elements split off and projected into others, and that it can be altered by identifications (both projective and introjective)

637

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Discussants as Psychoanalytic Readers

Stanley Coen

Stanley Coen

390 West End Avenue, IH
New York, NY 10024-6200
Fax: 212-362-0682
E-mail: Sjcoenmd@aol.com

Stanley Coen  

Psychoanalytic discussants of clinical papers face certain tasks, opportunities, and difficulties. To be willing to present and publish their analytic work for colleagues to study, analyst-authors and discussants need to expect that the other will respectfully complement his or her perspective. Discussants are encouraged to immerse themselves playfully in the affective force-field constituted between analysand and analyst within the process of the presentation. As discussants struggle to extricate themselves from this force-field, they contribute best by confining their understandings to the presentation process rather than trying to pin down the actual psychoanalytic situation. When this is done, discussants are under less pressure to inform presenters. This paper considers how discussants need to process their feeling responses to clinical papers so as to enhance rather than injure the analyst-author. This approach is illustrated with vignettes of the author's efforts as a clinical discussant to become a constructive psychoanalytic reader.

[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Implications of Current Attachment Theory

Stephen Seligman

Stephen Seligman

3667 Sacramento Street,
San Francisco, CA 94118
Fax: 415-648-9760
E-mail: Seligmn@itsa.ucsf.edu

Stephen Seligman   Faculty, San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute; Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Infant-Parent Program, University of California, San Francisco.

Current attachment theory suggests that security in the presence of another—that is, secure attachment—is part of the same psychic process as the ability to understand the distinction between one's own mind and the external world (Main 1995; Fonagy and Target 1996). This is a dramatic innovation with rich and varied implications for the psychoanalytic views of psychopathology, clinical technique, and therapeutic action; yet at the same time it converges with basic analytic values. It corresponds in focus remarkably closely to the everyday conduct of analysis, relying as it does on moment-to-moment efforts to understand the analysand's mind in an appropriately secure environment. In contrast, many other theories of therapeutic action require a kind of awkward translation from metapsychology to what analysts actually do. Inasmuch as current attachment researchers have now provided extensive empirical verification of their perspective, an exceptionally encouraging and stimulating convergence of developmental research and psychoanalytic thinking is now available, and is well illustrated in this panel.

Many of these innovations, including the ones reported upon here, rest on a crucial finding from the Adult Attachment Interview: it is the extent to which we can reflect coherently on our experiences, in both narrative and affective modes, that determines the level of security and organization in our psychic lives, more even than the experiences themselves.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Femininization1 of the Female Oedipal Complex, Part I1

A Reconsideration of the Significance of Separation Issues

Deanna Holtzman and Nancy Kulish

Nancy Kulish

625 Purdy
Birmingham, Ml 48009
Fax: 248-258-9348
E-mail: Nkulish@aol.com

Nancy Kulish  

Freud's insights about the oedipus complex have been universalized to include the psychology of the girl. The authors argue that this crucial developmental phase for girls has uniquely feminine characteristics that have not been fully recognized or cohesively incorporated into psychoanalytic theories. This paper addresses these differences, which are based on characteristic patterns of object relationships, typical defenses, and social considerations. The authors argue that “female oedipal” is an oxymoron, and propose that this constellation be named “the Persephone complex” after the Greek myth of Persephone, which seems to capture better the typical situation of the little girl. They focus on the issue of separation and its complicated and necessary role in the triangular situation of females. Using illustrations from clinical material, the authors argue that the frequent appearance of separation material linked to triangular heterosexual competitive fantasies can and should be differentiated from material in which ideas about separation stem from dyadic and earlier issues. Misunderstanding how these separation conflicts tie into triangular “oedipal” relationships can lead to a “preoedipalization” of the dynamics of girls and women.

There is an excerpt from the play Fires in the Mirror, by Anna Deavere Smith, that seems particularly relevant to the topic of attachment. It reads:

Identity…. it's psychic sense of place

It's a way of knowing I'm not that rock or tree?

I'm this other living creature over here?

And it's a way of knowing that no matter where I put myself

that I am not necessarily what's around me.

I am part of my surroundings

and I become separate from them

and it's being able to make those differentiations clearly

that lets us have an identity

and what's inside our identity

[[RunningHead]]

Barbie and the Tree of Life

The Multiple Functions of Gender in Development

Judith Yanof

Judith Yanof

25 Somerset Road
West Newton, MA 02465
Fax: 617-527-6150
E-mail: uLboden@bu.edu

Judith Yanof  

Gender identity is the lens through which people experience being boy or girl, man or woman. It is a complex compromise formation that is not separate from the wishes, fears, and intrapsychic conflicts of other domains. Over the course of development, gender identity becomes layered and reconfigured. This paper looks at one child's experience of gender over several phases in her development in order to learn how gender is integrated into identity. At different times, as different conflicts came to the fore, she used gender to shape and lend definition to these conflicts. Conversely, her experiences in other spheres influenced her experience of gender.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Countertransference, Conflictual Listening, and the Analytic Object Relationship

Henry Smith

Henry Smith

17 Hammond Street
Cambridge, MA 02138-1915
Fax: 617-354-3180
E-mail: Hfsmith l @compuserve.com

Henry Smith  

Analytic listening is an ongoing conflictual process, containing all the components of conflict and shaped in every moment by both the patient's and the analyst's conflicts. The mutual responsiveness that develops between analyst and patient stems from a complex conflictual object relationship, fundamentally no different from any other object relationship, in which countertransference at all times simultaneously facilitates and interferes with the analytic work. Detailed clinical process is used to illustrate these and related phenomena, including the use of signal conflict, the benign negative countertransference, the function of countertransference structures, and the analyst's use of projection. The analyst's affects, thoughts, and actions trace the shifting nature of the patient's transference and resistance, and the level of the object relationship continuously being created between patient and analyst.

[[RunningHead]]

The Organized Categories of Infant, Child, and Adult Attachment

Flexible vs. Inflexible Attention Under Attachment-Related Stress

Mary Main

Mary Main

3210 Tolman Hall
Berkeley, CA 94720
Fax: 510-642-5293

Mary Main   Professor of Psychology, University of California, Berkeley.

From an evolutionary perspective, a central mechanism promoting infant survival is the maintenance of proximity to attachment figures. Consequently attachment figure(s) represent the infant's primary solution to experiences of fear. Aspects of the development of the field of attachment are outlined within this context, beginning with Bowlby's ethological/evolutionary theory, and proceeding to Ainsworth's early descriptions of infant-mother interaction in Uganda and Baltimore. Using a laboratory procedure called the strange situation, Ainsworth identified three organized patterns of infant response to separation from and reunion with the parent. Narratives derived from videotaped strange situation behavior of infants in each category (secure, avoidant, and resistant/ambivalent) are provided, together with a discussion of the prototypical sequelae of each category (e.g., school behavior, and separation-related narratives and drawings at age six). The Adult Attachment Interview (AAI) and the move to the level of representation are also described. AAI transcripts are presently analyzed according to the speaker's capacity to adhere to Grice's maxims of rational cooperative discourse, and three organized AAI categories, or states of mind with respect to attachment, have been identified (secure-autonomous, dismissing, and preoccupied). When the interview is administered to parents who have

been seen with their infants in the strange situation, each AAI category has repeatedly been found to predict that infant's strange situation response to that parent. Illustrations of the discourse characteristic of each category are provided, and it is noted that individuals with apparently unfavorable life histories are found to have secure offspring, providing that their history is recounted coherently. Like infant strange situation behavior, differences in adult security as identified through discourse patterning are interpreted in terms of attentional flexibility or inflexibility under attachment-related stress.

Separation-Individuation Phenomena in Adulthood

General Concepts and the Fifth Individuation

Calvin Colarusso

Calvin Colarusso

1020 Prospect St., Suite 415B
La Jolla, CA 92037
Fax: 858-454-2473
E-mail: Calc@san.rr.com

Calvin Colarusso   Training and Supervising Analyst in Child and Adult Psychoanalysis, San Diego Psychoanalytic Institute; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, University of California at San Diego.

The elaboration of separation-individuation theory in the years beyond childhood has been a psychoanalytic focus for several decades. In this paper, the nature of separation-individuation phenomena in adulthood and the terminology and controversy surrounding the use of the terms third, fourth, and fifth individuation are considered first; a detailed elaboration of the concept of the fifth individuation in late adulthood follows.

[[RunningHead]]

Reapproaching Mahler

New Perspectives on Normal Autism, Symbiosis, Splitting and Libidinal Object Constancy from Cognitive Developmental Theory

György Gergely

György Gergely

P.O. Box 398.H-1394
Budapest
Hungary
Fax: +361-269-2972
E-mail: Gergelyg@mtapi.hu

György Gergely  

Margaret Mahler's seminal work on the psychological birth of the infant has generated more critical discussion than most other psychoanalytic theories of early development. But while the empirically, theoretically, and methodologically based criticisms of Mahler's theory are generally justified, her critics fail at times to consider some of the intriguing and creative insights that her theoretical formulations tried to capture. This paper attempts to reformulate these insights using the new conceptual tools that current cognitive developmental theory provides. Mahler's stage of normal autism is reconsidered in the light of contingency detection theory as an initial phase of primary preoccupation with self-generated perfectly response-contingent stimulation. Her concept of normal symbiosis is recast with the help of attachment theory's views on homeostatic regulation and the social biofeedback model of affect-reflective mirroring interactions with parents. Finally, her ideas about the development of splitting and libidinal object constancy are reconsidered according to recent theories of early representational development and mentalization.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

JAPA 2000

Arnold Richards

Arnold Richards

Arnold Richards  

This issue is the first of a new year, and of a new millennium. We begin the new century mindful that the last issue of the last century—Between the Millennia: Freud and Psychoanalysis—will be a hard act to follow. We are grateful for the feedback, mostly positive, that we have received about 47/4 so far. The contents, and the cover, sparked worldwide interest and controversy that I think bode well for psychoanalytic journal publication and scholarship.

With this issue, abstracts of the scientific papers in six languages will be posted on our website four to six weeks before the issue's mailing date. The full text of editorials, commentaries, letters and selected contributions will be “published” on our site simultaneously with the print edition. The site includes a direct link to the editor, and we hope that readers will use it freely to offer comments pro and con on past, present, and future issues.

We bid farewell and thanks to the editorial board members and associates who completed their service in December, and welcome to the board six new members elected this past May to three-year terms.

Dale Boesky is a senior statesman of American psychoanalysis. Former editor of psychoanalytic Quarterly and a popular discussant for panels and papers, he is associated with a classical perspective but is a thoughtful student who is always fair-minded in his consideration of other approaches.

Robert Glick, director of the Columbia Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research, is a training analyst and professor of clinical psychiatry. Former program chair for the Association for Psychoanalytic Medicine, he currently serves on the Board on Professional Standards and the Program Committee of the American Psychoanalytic Association. He has published twenty papers, chapters, and book reviews and had coedited five books, including volumes on masochism, affect, aggression, and anxiety.

responseSet.title: On Gratitude and Gratification
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Gratitude and Gratification

Glen Gabbard

Glen Gabbard

P. O. Box 829
Topeka, KS 66601-0829
Fax: 785-272-9577
E-mail: Gabbargo@menninger.edu

Glen Gabbard  

Expressions of gratitude from the patient may be regarded by the analyst as a much-needed validation or affirmation of competence. The analyst's need for gratitude may be a relatively silent presence when things are going smoothly or when the analyst's efforts are openly appreciated by the patient. Ungrateful patients, however, are likely to force the analyst to confront his or her unconscious background wish to enact a longed-for mode of relatedness as part of the daily work of psychoanalysis. The analyst's wish for a specific form of object relationship involving a selfless, devoted helper and an appreciative patient who acknowledges having been helped may be thwarted by certain patients at every turn, knowing they are depriving the analyst of a particular form of gratification in their work. For patients of this type, failure may mean success. The implications of this particular form of clinical stalemate are outlined, and a clinical example illustrates some of the challenges encountered in psychoanalytic work with ungrateful patients.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the Receiving End

Facilitating the Analysis of Conflicted Drive Derivatives of Aggression

Paul Gray

Paul Gray

3315 Wisconsin Avenue NW
Washington, DC 20016-3800
E-mail: Paulgray@erols.com

Paul Gray  

Freud's delay in giving aggression an instinctual role comparable to that of libido had clinical effects. Historically, this delay rescued psychoanalysis and its practitioners from destruction by Austria's anti-Semitic leadership. Since today the cultural threat to analysis is of quite a different nature, methods are proposed for making drive derivatives of aggression (and, in turn, libidinal drive derivatives) more accessible. Certain traditional methods have in significant degree distracted analysts from techniques that more effectively allow patients to bring conflicted derivatives of aggression into nonneurotic management through analytically facilitated ego growth. Approaches that move away from the concept of conflicted instinctual drives compound the basic resistance to analyzing defended drive derivatives of aggression. A recommendation is made for a two-tier choice of method: (1) a traditional technique for patients needing relatively less access to their aggression to achieve a satisfying outcome, and (2) close process attention for the analysis of analytic candidates and others for whom it is professionally advantageous to achieve greater insight into their aggressive potential. Recommendations are made regarding how the analyst can attain the neutrality necessary to conduct analyses of the latter sort without having to suffer narcissistic trauma while on the receiving end of verbally explicit, undisplaced drive derivatives of aggression.

[[RunningHead]]

The Case History

Robert Michels

Robert Michels

418 East 71st Street #41
New York, NY 10021
Fax: 212-746-3828
E-mail: Rmichels@med.cornell.edu

Robert Michels  

Case histories report the basic data of psychoanalysis, and yet they are strikingly infrequent in the psychoanalytic literature. This reflects not only the methodological problems in describing psychoanalytic cases, but also the complex intentions of psychoanalysts who want both to present and to conceal aspects of their work. The history of the case history is discussed, as are the nature and purpose of case reports, the differences between oral and written reports, the relation between case reports and psychoanalyses, and case reports as countertransferential enactments. A plea is made for including authorial intention as an explicit part of the report, rather than concealing it behind a scientific facade.

[[RunningHead]]

Commentary

David Tuckett

David Tuckett

Gower Street
London WCI 6BT
Fax +44 20 7916 8502
Email: D.Tuckett@ucl.ac.uk

David Tuckett  

Robert Michels's skillful and incisive discussion of the history of the psychoanalytic case history exposes, in the manner in which we are accustomed to expect from him, the still chaotic state of our intentions when seeking to draw conclusions from what we tell each other we do.

403

Michels's review is exemplary and complements an earlier effort by Widlöcher (1994), alongside which I found it interesting to read—partly as an interesting comparison of French and American views of the issues. Widlöcher, interestingly, as he occupied Charcot's position at the Salpêtriére, focuses less on the issue of how far clinical reporting can “prove” anything and more on the issue of how clinicians can learn from experience. The difference in emphasis is relevant to Widlöcher's academic post. It has been argued that it was upon Freud's visit to Charcot at the Salpêtriére that he learned to value and interpret clinical data in a way rather at odds with the approach in the German-speaking, physiologically based medicine of the day (Schwartz 1999, p. 38). As Freud (1893) reported, Charcot said, “La theorie c'est bon, mais ça n'empeche pas d'exister. [Theory is good, but it doesn't prevent the existence of other things]” (p. 13). The tension between the difficulty of using “mere” clinical experience to advance knowledge and the necessity of doing so seems to me to live on and to be beautifully caught in Michels's address, as it is in current uncertainties about how psychoanalysts should establish confidence in their ideas. However, my main aim in this commentary is to take up four of the main points Michels has raised and to consider them a bit further.

Science. The first issue is the question of how we regard clinical data. Michels demonstrates the severe difficulty in drawing any secure conclusions from the clinical material we have accumulated so far in our discipline. He raises the question of what can constitute “scientific” data and how the various “selections” typically involved in forms of psychoanalytic clinical presentations can meet “scientific” criteria. Avoiding scorn, Michels demonstrates in detail the limitations of efforts to date and the failures to move forward over the past one hundred years. I agree fully.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Primary Femininity

Clinical Advances and Theoretical Ambiguities

Nancy Kulish

Nancy Kulish

625 Purdy
Birmingham, MI 48009
Fax: 248-258-9348
Nkulish@aol.com

Nancy Kulish  

This paper examines the use of the term “primary femininity” in current psychoanalytic thinking. The concept of primary feminity arose in reaction to early theories about female sexuality and development; based on a model of male development, these presented problems when applied to females. The author attempts to demonstrate the clinical advances that have resulted from the idea of primary femininity. At the same time she argues that the idea has been used to carry widely differing meanings, and has reflected many writers' differing frames of reference, which range from gender identity through biological traits, object relations, genital anxieties, and bisexuality. Like the terms it originally was intended to replace or augment, it has come to be used reductionistically or loosely. The author warns against its misuse and argues that primary femininity is not a unitary concept, but rather encompasses a related group of ideas about the female body and mind.

[[RunningHead]]

Letting Go

Some Thoughts About Termination

Shelley Orgel

Shelley Orgel

245 East 87th Street
New York, NY 10128
E-mail: Shelleyorgel@aol.com

Shelley Orgel  

Termination of analysis is discussed from three perspectives. First, considered as a vicissitude of the analytic relationship, termination contains essential elements of the psychoanalytic process itself. Cycles of attachment, loss, mourning, and internalization mark moments in, as well as overviews of, every analysis from its beginning to well past its termination. Second, Freud's approach to the subject of termination is explored and widened, with an emphasis on its relation to mourning and on the depth and permanence of analytic transference—two dimensions relatively neglected by Freud, perhaps for personal reasons. Finally, clinical issues are presented that are meaningful to the author in his work with analysands, including his work as a training analyst.

[[RunningHead]]

A Psychoanalytic Perspective on Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder

Karen Gilmore

Karen Gilmore

305 East 18th Street
New York, NY 10003
Fax: 212-473-8662
E-mail: Kgilmore@psychoanalysis.net

Karen Gilmore   Training and Supervising Analyst, New York Psychoanalytic Institute and Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research. Chair, Child Psychoanalysis Program and Director, Parent-Infant Program, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research

Psychoanalysts have tended to view the diagnosis of AD/HD either with skepticism or as a contraindication to analytic treatment. The author reviews the history of this puzzling diagnostic entity, which is estimated to account for up to fifty percent of child referrals, and suggests that a psychoanalytic perspective on the underlying disturbance in ego functioning helps to clarify the nature of the symptom picture. Whatever the etiology, which may be compound, she suggests that psychoanalytic treatment, most often in conjunction with psychopharmacological therapy, can address the core disturbance in ego integration that creates the familiar triad of inattention, impulsivity, and hyperactivity.

[[RunningHead]]

Modern Defense Analysis

Three Perspectives on the Same Hours

James Hansell

James Hansell

425 East Washington Street
Suite 202-S
Ann Arbor, MI 48104
E-mail: Jhansell@umich.edu

James Hansell  

Fred Busch opened the panel by noting that the idea for it had come from the observation that in many respects all schools of psycho-analysis view defenses similarly. Defenses are generally understood as self-protective measures, and it is generally agreed that they are best addressed analytically on the basis of mutually observable facts about the patient's behavior in analysis. Given this consensus, why is there then such disparity in the way that analysts from different schools actually work with defenses? Busch hoped that the panel might help to address this question.

The second impetus for the panel came from Busch's experience talking with other analysts about his view of defense analysis. Frequently, he noted, colleagues will respond, “We've always worked that way.” However, he has observed that their clinical techniques are in fact quite different from what he is describing.

The panelists were chosen to allow comparisons among different perspectives on defense analysis. Each was asked to comment on three clinical hours of the work of an experienced analyst. The panelists were asked to describe how they would work with this material, and how their approach might differ from the approach to defense analysis evident in the clinical hours.

Busch introduced the clinical material, which was from the third year of the analysis of a married man in his thirties working with a senior analyst. The patient, Phillip, had presented with long-standing depression and anxiety and a fall-off in productivity in his academic position following a stellar graduate school career. He had a tendency to “shoot himself in the foot” and antagonize others.

Panel held at the Spring Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Washington, DC, May 16, 1999. Panelists: Fred Busch (chair), Steven Cooper, Manuel Furer, Dorothy Holmes.

What is a Deep Interpretation?

Fred Busch

Fred Busch

246 Eliot Street
Brookline, MA 02467
E-mail: Fredbusch@aol.com

Fred Busch  

Important differences are emerging regarding the place where analysts believe the most meaningful analytic work takes place. One area that highlights these distinct ways of working is the analyst's view of deep interpretations. Models underlying the differing perspectives on this issue are presented, along with an extended clinical example that illustrates the importance of considering, in formulating analytic interventions, the concept of a structured mind. A view of the analytic process that accords the patient's perspective greater privilege is introduced.

responseSet.title: “The Write Stuff”
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“The Write Stuff”

Lynn Reiser

Lynn Reiser

Suite 300A
345 Whitney Avenue
New Haven, CT 06511
E-mail: Lynn.reiser@yale.edu

Lynn Reiser  

In 1930, Freud received the Goethe Prize for literature. In the years after Freud, the ability to write about clinical material has been taken for granted. For most analysts, psychoanalytic writing has been regarded as incidental to their professional activity, a skill acquired by trial and error, put to use in process notes and summaries during training; later, by some, in case write-ups for certification; by even fewer, in case studies prepared for presentation or publication; or, by a very few, for research.

Many analysts actively avoid writing. Yet the time-consuming nature of analytic work, and in consequence the small number of patients any analyst can treat, has always mandated that analysts learn from each other's work. Effective writing about clinical process is currently being recognized as a matter of high importance for progress in theory and research in psychoanalysis, as well as for technical advances in psychoanalytic practice. Several papers in this issue of JAPA examine why clinical writing is important, what constitutes effective writing, and why analysts often do not write. The papers address the complications and complexities that emerge as these issues are confronted.

Writing about the analytic process might sound straightforward—but it is not a simple matter. To those who have never tried it, psychoanalysis itself might seem a simple task, for both patient and analyst. Although the patient has merely to say “whatever comes to mind,” it quickly becomes evident that this is not possible. The patient is perceptually informed by input from within and without the body. Mental content is nonverbal as well as verbal, often vague and fleeting, not organized in a coherent way even if it is verbal, and if coherent still idiosyncratic and largely incomprehensible to a listener ignorant of the referents.

The novice analysand discovers that even the “simple” task of recounting a dream is not simple. The remembered dream is likely to feel vague in comparison to the vividness of the experienced dream.

responseSet.title: Disorganized Infant, Child, and Adult Attachment: Collapse in Behavioral and Attentional Strategies
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Disorganized Infant, Child, and Adult Attachment

Collapse in Behavioral and Attentional Strategies

Erik Hesse and Mary Main

Mary Main

3210 Tolman Hall
Berkeley CA 94720
Fax: 510-642-5293

Mary Main  

This presentation focuses on the disorganized/disoriented (Group D) categories of infant, child, and adult attachment. The infant D category is assigned on the basis of interruptions and anomalies in organization and orientation observed during Ainsworth's strange situation procedure. In neurologically normal low-risk samples, D attachment is not substantially related to descriptions of infant temperament, and usually appears with respect to only one parent. At six, former D infants are often found to be role-inverting (D-Controlling) towards the parent, while drawings and separation-related narratives (D-Fearful) suggest continuing states of fear and disorganization. In adults, marked lapses in reasoning and discourse surrounding the discussion of loss or abuse during the Adult Attachment Interview (AAI) causes a transcript to be assigned to Unresolved/disorganized (U/d) adult attachment status, which predicts infant D attachment. Bowlby's theory is extended, with the proposal that certain forms of frightening parental behavior will arouse contradictory biologically channeled propensities to approach and to take flight from the parent. Maltreated infants are therefore highly likely to be disorganized. Also identified are subtler forms of frightening parental behavior (including dissociative behavior and anomalous forms of frightened behavior) that appear to lead to infant disorganization. This suggests that infant D attachment may at times represent a second-generation effect of the parent's own continuing unresolved responses to trauma. Infant D attachment predicts disruptive/aggressive and dissociative disorders in childhood and adolescence, while U/d adult attachment appears frequently in psychiatric and criminal populations. Clinical implications are discussed.

[[RunningHead]]

The Analyst's Witnessing and Otherness

Warren Poland

Warren Poland

5225 Connecticut Avenue
Washington, DC 20015
Fax: 301-229-0333
E-mail: Wspmd@erols.com

Warren Poland  

The analyst's active though silent witnessing of the patient's self-inquiry is presented as an essential aspect of the analytic process. Witnessing, though rooted in the analyst's empathy and holding, represents a more advanced development of those functions based on relational maturation from union to self-other differentiation. Self-definition and regard for otherness are seen as intrinsically unitary. Psychoanalytic witnessing is first illustrated and defined, then located as a derivative of negation in the unfolding of the analytic process, next considered in relation to current concerns for intersubjectivity, and finally linked to current shifts in philosophical thought.

At the core of the being of each person there is a solitude in which he is related to himself…. The ground of genuine analytic work in the analyst is his attitude of respect for this solitude.

Charles Hanly
