[[RunningHead]]

Attachment and Borderline Personality Disorder

Peter Fonagy

Peter Fonagy

Gower Street
London WCIE 6BT
P.fonagy@ucl.ac.uk

Peter Fonagy  

The author outlines his concept of reflective function or mentalization, which is defined as the capacity to think about mental states in oneself and in others. He presents evidence to suggest that the capacity for reflective awareness in a child's caregiver increases the likelihood of the child's secure attachment, which in turn facilitates the development of mentalization in the child. He proposes that a secure attachment relationship offers the child a chance to explore the mind of the caregiver, and in this way to learn about minds; he formulates this model of the birth of the psychological self as a variation on the Cartesian cogito: “My caregiver thinks of me as thinking and therefore I exist as a thinker” This model is then applied to provide insight into some personality-disordered individuals who were victims of childhood abuse. The author proposes (1) that individuals who experience early trauma may defensively inhibit their capacity to mentalize to avoid having to think about their caregiver's wish to harm them; and (2) that some characteristics of severe borderline personality disorder may be rooted in developmental pathology associated with this inhibition. He offers evidence for and some qualifications of this model, and argues that the therapeutic effect of psychoanalysis depends on its capacity to activate patients' ability to evolve an awareness of mental states and thus find meaning in their own and other people's behavior.

responseSet.title: The Development and Organization of Attachment: Implications for Psychoanalysis
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Development and Organization of Attachment

Implications for Psychoanalysis

Arietta Slade

Arietta Slade

8 Hodge Road
Roxbury, CT 06783
Fax: 860-350-2570
E-mail: Aslade@snet.net

Arietta Slade   Professor of Clinical and Developmental Psychology, Graduate School and University Center of the City University of New York; Research Consultant, Parent-Infant Program, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research

As a result of John Bowlby's breach with the British Psychoanalytic Society nearly forty years ago, his work, specifically the development of attachment theory, was until recently largely expunged from the psychoanalytic record. However, thanks to developments in both psychoanalytic and attachment theories, a rapprochement has been forged, and a number of scholars are now seeking to integrate these two complementary perspectives. In this paper, the fundamental premises of attachment theory are discussed in light of their relation to psychoanalytic theory. In addition, their application to the clinical situation in both adult and child treatment is discussed.

The Lobo-Cabernite Affair

Helena Vianna

Helena Vianna

Rua Anita Garibaldi 29 apto. 1001
Rio de Janeiro 22041–080
Brazil
Fax: +55–21-549–4346
E-mail: Helbes@openlink.com.br

Helena Vianna  

December 1, 1999. I wish to clarify certain points in Robert Wallerstein's commentary on Janine Puget's review of my book Psychoanalysis, Dictatorship, and Torture (JAPA 47/3,

About Amilcar Lobo. (1) We all know that the job of army psychiatrist that Wallerstein attributes to Lobo did not involve work exactly proper to a psychiatrist. (2) The general headquarters on Barão de Mesquita in Rio de Janeiro, where Lobo “worked” with a torture squad, was not simply a “military detention center.” Its cellars and secret rooms were designed specifically for the use of the most sophisticated methods for torturing political prisoners. This fact was not unknown to the public, although in the 1970s it was forbidden to talk or write about the torture of political prisoners. (3) Lobo worked as a military doctor of his own free will, given that his regular job as a doctor serving in the armed forces had terminated in 1972. Most likely, both Antonio Dutra, his first analyst, and then Leã Cabernite knew this, as Lobo in fact confirms in his autobiography. (4) Lobo was not just “an army psychiatrist complicit in the torture” (p. 965); he was in fact and directly a torturer. This was established particularly by the declarations of political prisoners at Brazilian Bar Association (OAB) hearings. The proceedings of those hearings were forwarded to the IPA Council in 1981 by the president of the OAB, but never received any response, although this was explicitly requested. Why was this?

About the participation of Cabernite and IPA officers. (1)

[[RunningHead]]

The Lobo-Cabernite Affair

Janine Puget

Janine Puget

Paraguay 2475
1121 Buenos Aires
Argentina
E-mail: Janine@pccp.com.ar

Janine Puget  

April 18, 2000. I am grateful to JAPA for giving me the opportunity to revisit such an important matter as the Rio I, or Lobo-Cabernite, affair. From my point of view, this matter involves much more for the IPA than simply Rio I, even though it has a specific weight for Brazilian psychoanalysts. It involves not simply an ethical problem, but one fraught with sociopolitical significance. It seems evident that our institutions are not prepared to take into account and solve, as quickly as is necessary, this kind of ethical problem, perhaps because we know more about the analytic setting than about the institutional and political setting. The Lobo-Cabernite problem has been puzzling IPA officers since the very beginning, when we first began to know what was going on in Rio I. As tends to happen in this kind of serve ethical deviation, we all knew, but it was an ineffective knowledge. The same happened in Germany and in other countries where racial, ethnic, or religious differences have led to oppression and atrocities, and consequently to some very shameless decisions by psychoanalytic institutions. People knew but did not want to know, and so did not act in accord with the ethics of our profession.

Now we know much more. Rio I commands attention from JAPA, a concern that shows itself in the fact that my book review was thought to merit a response in the very issue in which it appeared. Now Helena Besserman Vianna answers Robert Wallerstein's commentary in JAPA 47/3, and Wallerstein again responds. JAPA has become a publication that enables us to discuss publicly the sort of ethical problems raised by Rio I; the present discussion sheds lights on the inability of IPA officers to resolve this kind of problem.

1036

responseSet.title: Infertility in the Age of Technology
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Infertility in the Age of Technology

Sharon Zalusky

Sharon Zalusky

11980 San Vicente Blvd.
Suite 904
Los Angeles, CA 90049
Szalusky@aol.com.

Sharon Zalusky   Faculty, Southern California Psychoanalytic Institute.

Rapid advances in medical and biological technologies are changing the ways we are born live, and die. Biotechnology is pushing us in our notion of what is possible, and tapping into our most primitive omnipotent fantasies. For the most part these new techniques have not yet been integrated into a shared social consciousness. This paper examines, in a case study of a woman who eventually got pregnant with a donor egg and in a shorter case vignette, how the new technology impacts upon the analytic process. Attention is focused on the permeability of the boundaries between analyst and patient and between fantasy and action. The study shows how analyst and patient, together and often for the first time, must face the difficult moral and ethical issues stimulated by such procedures, as well as the anxieties and underlying fantasies they evoke. It is the uniqueness and the intensity of this experience that permeates boundaries, stimulates emotion in patient and analyst alike, and has the potential to deepen the analytic bond. The study underlines the need for the analyst to be flexible, moving back and forth between interpreting and creating a needed holding environment. Finally, the paper points out the tension between the traditional roles of motherhood, fatherhood, and family and those being created in this high-tech world.

[[RunningHead]]

Passion, Countertransference Enactment, and Breakdown in the Psychoanalysis of a Young Woman

Peter Wegner

Peter Wegner

Pfalzhaldenweg 16
D–72070 Tübingen
Germany
Fax: +49-7071-43919
E-mail: Wegner@t-online.de

Peter Wegner  

Transference-countertransference involvement with patients the analyst finds “pleasurable” is examined. Analysis of such patients courts the danger that pathological identifications will go unrecognized and the analysis end at a point where the opportunity for decisive change, which to the patient appears physically and psychically catastrophic, is forgone. A presentation of one aspect of the analysis of a young woman reveals the psychic pain experienced by analysand and analyst when the feared breakdown places a pathological identification in doubt, and the difficulties that can ensue in treatment. Other psychodynamic lines of interpretation and differential diagnostic considerations are put aside in favor of an examination of the special role of enactment and countertransference enactment and their impact in promoting development or strengthening defense.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Patient-Therapist Match

Revelation or Resistance?

Susan Vaughan and Steven Roose

Steven Roose

25 West 81st Street, Suite IC
New York, NY 10024
Fax: 212-721-0408
E-mail: Scvl@columbia.edu

Steven Roose  

Patient-therapist match is a relatively new yet frequently invoked concept within psychoanalysis. Despite Freud's appreciation of the influence of the analyst's past to his or her work within the analytic setting, psychoanalysts have historically held varied opinions about the degree to which the analyst's personality and conflicts affect the analytic process. As analysis was reconfigured as a two-person system, attention focused on the fit between patient and analyst. The literature on patient-therapist match is reviewed, and the conclusion reached that this intuitively appealing concept suffers from a lack of rigorous definition and operationalization. Many authors invoke match in ways that imply that it is real, static, external to the domain of analytic inquiry, and unaffected by analytic process. In its present form, the concept of patient-therapist match obstructs rather than facilitates analytic exploration and obscures rather than clarifies what happens between analyst and analysand in psychoanalysis. By suggesting that match exists as a reality outside the domain of transference and countertransference, analysts may overlook the importance of psychoanalytic technique in creating a sense of match. Analysts may attribute stalemated or limited analyses to a bad match, rather than tenaciously exploring the transference-countertransference configurations that remain at the heart of analytic work.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis, Development, and the Life Cycle
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis, Development, and the Life Cycle

Phyllis Tyson

Phyllis Tyson

Phyllis Tyson  

This issue of JAPA focuses on psychic development and the life cycle. It demonstrates the continuing psychoanalytic concern with various aspects of the emerging inner world of the young child and the ways in which this influences later psychic functioning. Papers included cover venues ranging from the consulting rooms of child analysts to the laboratories of empirical researchers: the places where important work is elucidating these matters. Also included are the clinical challenges faced by the analyst of adults who turns to developmental theories to find meaning; in doing so an analyst risks confusion, as concepts constructed to explain the psychology of the young child may lose specificity and meaning when applied to the psychology of the adult. Finally, included here as well are examples of the ways that theories derived from child analysis or empirical developmental research can be used, and extended to adult phenomena that may be at least descriptively similar to childhood ones.

The present edition of JAPA is notable in several respects. For one, child psychoanalysis has a voice. In her report of the psychoanalysis of a young girl, Yanof defies those skeptical about the process of child analysis, while at the same time challenging us to reconsider certain central developmental theories. The clinical material of Aruffo, Ibarra, and Strupp is a welcome demonstration of the ways in which child psychoanalysis can and must challenge current pediatric and psychiatric notions about certain childhood syndromes. Gilmore similarly demonstrates ways in which psychoanalytic treatment of a child, in conjunction with psychopharmacological therapy, can address the core disturbance in ego integration associated with the diagnosis of Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder.

Of particular importance in this volume is the focus on needed dialogue between psychoanalysis and attachment theory.

1045

responseSet.title: Intersubjectivity and the Philosophical Tradition
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Intersubjectivity and the Philosophical Tradition

Roger Frie

Roger Frie

1530 16th Street NW 402
Washington, DC 20036

Roger Frie  

September 4, 1999. I was pleased that Robert Stolorow and Donna Orange found much that they were in sympathy with in their review of my Subjectivity and Intersubjectivity in Modern Philosophy and Psychoanalysis (JAPA 47/2). However, in drawing a distinction between their own conception of intersubjectivity and my discussion of the topic, they misconstrue some of the book's central arguments, which I hope to clarify here.

684

Stolorow and Orange suggest that I equate intersubjectivity with language. In fact, this is precisely what I dispute. I argue at length against the postmodern trend in philosophy and psychoanalysis, which sees subjectivity only as a product of the language we speak. For many postmodern theorists, subjectivity follows from our linguistic relation to others. As a result, linguistic intersubjectivity is taken as a starting point and subjectivity is construed in relation to it.

I argue, in contrast, that linguistic conceptions of intersubjectivity do not account for prelinguistic and nonverbal forms of experience. The point is that intersubjective experience always includes the affective and bodily dimensions, which are central to psychoanalysis but cannot be reduced to language. I also question whether a linguistically constituted theory of the subject can account sufficiently for individual identity and personal agency. In considering the importance of individual subjectivity, I maintain that agency cannot be seen as only an epiphenomenon of language or larger social forms. And I question whether subjectivity is itself entirely a function of intersubjectivity.

Drawing on a line of thought from Fichte and Schelling through Sartre and contemporary German theory, I elaborate individual subjectivity in terms of a basic awareness of self that is prepersonal and prereflective and actually enables self-reflection to take place. I thus draw a distinction between prereflective awareness of self, reminiscent of Winnicott's “private self,” and the “I” of reflective self-consciousness. However, Stolorow and Orange misunderstand my discussion when they claim that I endorse the “Sartrean view that we all begin as Cartesian isolated minds.” Not only does Sartre argue at length against Descartes's cogito, the theory of individual subjectivity I elaborate is manifestly post-Cartesian. That is, I seek to account both for the centrality of individual subjectivity and for interpersonal relatedness, which together constitute the human condition.

Letters

Leon Hoffman

Leon Hoffman

167 East 67th Street
New York, NY 10021
Fax: 212–327-0640
E-mail: 73542.334@compuserve.com

Leon Hoffman  

The Exclusion of Child Psychoanalysis

August 29, 2000. In a recent article, Arnold Richards (1999) discusses the negative impact for psychoanalysis of the politics of exclusion. In his paper Richards himself, perhaps unwittingly, excludes from his consideration the exclusion of child psychoanalysis from mainstream psychoanalytic discourse. He has only one reference to child analysis, stating that “Brill [despite his opposition to nonmedical psychoanalytic practitioners] sent his two children, Edwin and Joia, to lay analysts for treatment” (p. 12).

It is striking how many analysts, including child psychoanalysts, “view child psychoanalysis as ‘derivative’ or ‘applied’ psychoanalysis” (Rosenblitt 2000) and omit child psychoanalytic insights from theoretical discussions. For example, there are virtually no references to the child analytic literature in the current psychoanalytic debate involving differences between the so-called one-person and two-person psychologies (Hoffman 1998), nor in Paul Gray's (1982, 1996) central contributions to the development of defense analysis with adults.

I would like to suggest that future clinical and theoretical psychoanalytic discussions take into account the seminal work of child psychoanalytic thinkers. For example, one might consider whether an early unacknowledged version of the one-person/two-person debate occurred in the 1920s, as child analysis was developing. Melanie Klein (1927) espoused the idea that in analytic work with children the child's relationship to the outside world was not significant, and thus reality issues and work with the parents were unnecessary and corrupting factors in a child's analysis. Anna Freud (1927) maintained, in contradistinction to Klein, the importance of the parents to the child, as superego figures and auxiliary ego figures. In her recommendations for analytic technique, she stressed that the analyst needed to form an alliance with the child so the child could trust the analyst, as well as establish a working alliance with the parents who needed to support the analysis emotionally and realistically. One resolution to this conflict was accomplished by the development of defense analysis with children, perhaps an unacknowledged forerunner of Paul Gray's conceptualizations in adult analysis.

[[RunningHead]]

On Writing from Clinical Experience

Jill Scharff

Jill Scharff

6612 Kennedy Drive
Chevy Chase MD 20815-6504
Fax 301-951-6335
E-mail: iiort@mindspring.com

Jill Scharff  

Pepers that present the life of the analytic session offer material through which analysts can together study analytic process and therapeutic action and arrive at consensus on how to improve psychoanalytic theory and practice. But some analysts have been deterred from publishing clinical material of that kind because of concerns about preserving confidentiality, protecting the therapeutic relationship, reporting accurately, being scrutinized, worrying about losing their colleagues' support, and not feeling authorized to present their views. Here conscious, preconscious, and unconscious constraints against writing and publishing are explored, and an example is given of successful self-analysis of a writing inhibition. The debate over the ethics of writing is reviewed and an argument made that detailed clinical description is useful in advancing analytic understanding. Finally, a clinical example shows how the analysand usefully analyzes the experience of reading what the analyst has written, and how the analyst's self-analysis may be promoted in resonance with the analysand's experience.

[[RunningHead]]

Commentary

André Green

André Green

9 avenue de l'Observatoire
75006 Paris
France
E-mail: AndreGreen@compuserve.com

André Green  

In the analytic situation two parties meet and communicate. The outcome of this exchange depends on both parties. On the side of the analysand our efforts are limited to cases that appear to be “analyzable.” But on the other hand, on the side of the analyst, various styles can be encountered. This may serve not only to remind us of the personal equation of each analyst, but to occasion our reflection on the different attitudes defined by varying strategies regarding the direction of the cure. Such strategies depend on one's conception of the psychoanalytic process. This debate is no longer only theoretical, as it is now widely accepted that the analyst's basic attitude may influence the course and result of an analysis.

Warren Poland's paper is itself an act of witnessing and otherness. Reading his work, I could not help thinking about it as his way of evaluating some of the directions of today's analytic literature and, maybe, to stand aside after having thought of the different streams that compose it.

To understand the current situation, it is necessary to have an historical overview of the different ways in which the analytic relationship has been conceived. We have indirect evidence that Freud himself was an authoritarian personality; this is obvious before the birth of psychoanalysis, as is evident in Studies on Hysteria, and even after he discovered the psychoanalytic method. Freud's writings show that he did not himself display the attitude of neutrality he advocated. Here the reader need only be reminded of Freud's case of the young homosexual girl, though other examples may be cited.

57

[[RunningHead]]

The Therapeutic and Working Alliances

Robert Hausner

Robert Hausner

2354 Post Street
San Francisco, CA 94115
E-mail: Rhausnermd@aol.com

Robert Hausner  

Since their introduction, the concepts of the therapeutic alliance and the working alliance have provoked debate regarding the nature and function of these alliances and the applicability and validity of the concepts. Features of these concepts as originally put forth by Zetzel and Greenson, respectively, are delineated, with emphasis on the significant distinctions between them. Their relation to degree of psychopathology is examined, especially with respect to what may be understood as the more “silent” aspects of the therapeutic alliance. Mutual identification, empathy, and role-responsiveness are stressed as constituent features of the therapeutic alliance, with the working alliance seen as possible (theoretically and clinically) only after a therapeutic alliance has to some degree been established. Both alliances are understood as intrinsic structures within the analytic process, and illustrative case material is presented.

[[RunningHead]]

Report from Abroad

A Letter from Greece

Peter Hartocollis

Peter Hartocollis

Sparoza, Paiania
190 02 Athens
Greece
Fax: +30 1 664–3647

Peter Hartocollis  

In his letter from Portugal (JAPA 47/2), our colleague Pedro Luzes noted that in 1915 the neuropsychiatrist Egas Moniz became the first to introduce Freud's theories to that country. Decades later he would receive the Nobel prize for his discovery of lobotomy, thought for a time to be the ultimate treatment for schizophrenia. The same year as in Portugal, Freud's theory was presented in Greece for the first time by a classical scholar and linguist, Manolis Triandaphyllidis. Many years later, in 1938, Triandaphyllidis published the first scientific grammar of modern Greek, which contributed decisively to the solution of a linguistic schizophrenia—the existence of two languages, the archaic or purist and the vernacular or demotic—that had for centuries bedeviled Greek society. Triandaphyllidis gave a concise description of psychoanalysis, explaining the theory of the unconscious, which, as Freud did initially, he called the subconscious, and the basic defense mechanisms of repression, projection, and sublimation. He had become acquainted with psychoanalysis during his postgraduate studies in Munich, and maintained a lively interest in it to the end of his creative life. In his archives is a postcard from Freud, dated February 27, 1920, in which the father of psychoanalysis addresses him as “dear colleague.”

Much more elaborate than Triandaphyllidis's presentation is an essay published in 1924 by Yannis Imvriotis, a psychologist who had studied in Germany and Paris. Presented in weekly sessions to the same learned society where Triandaphyllidis had given his lecture on Freud a decade earlier, it covers theoretical principles, dream interpretation, and therapeutic technique, the last illustrated with clinical examples. A year later, the parapsychologist and short story writer Angelos Tanagras published an article about psychoanalysis in his journal Psychic Research. A diploma honoring the founder of the science of the unconscious, signed by Tanagras, figures prominently on a wall of Freud's apartment in Vienna.

[[RunningHead]]

The Wish to Regress in Patient and Analyst

Stanley Coen

Stanley Coen

390 West End Avenue, Suite IH
New York, NY 10024-6200
Fax: 212-362-0682
E-mail: SJCoenMD@aol.com

Stanley Coen  

The analyst's wish to regress is used as a paradigm of the “forbidden” topic of what analysts want from their analysands. The aim is to expand the subjective domain of analysts' awareness so that they can analyze better by grasping more of their temptations with patients before enactment can occur. Clinical examples illustrate how the author temporarily joined patients in wish-fulfilling mutual regression. Analytic process is disrupted when the analyst wishes to relinquish the more differentiated role of the containing and interpreting analyst in favor of more childlike relatedness both with the patient and with the analyst's internal objects. The author, expecting a more typical countertransference, had not anticipated that he might temporarily join these nonpsychotic patients in mutual regression. It is suggested that in the face of analytic impasse analysts should consider whether they might temporarily have joined the patient in mutually regressive wishes that have taken them away from more responsible analytic functioning.

Suggestion and Psychoanalytic Technique

Steven Levy and Lawrence Inderbitzin

Lawrence Inderbitzin

Emory University Psychoanalytic Institute

Lawrence Inderbitzin  

The role of the analyst's suggestive influence on the course and outcome of psychoanalytic treatment is explored, and traditional and newer perspectives on analytic technique are contrasted. The intersubjective critique of the neutral, objective analyst in relation to suggestion is examined. The inevitable presence and need for suggestive factors in analysis, and the relationship of suggestion to transference susceptibility, are emphasized. The manner in which the analysis of suggestive factors is subsumed in transference analysis as part of traditional technique is highlighted.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Older Patient in Psychoanalysis

Arthur Valenstein

Arthur Valenstein

140 Foster Street
Cambridge, MA 02138-4746
E-mail: Arthur_valenstein@hms.harvard.edu

Arthur Valenstein   Training and Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Institute of New England, East: Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Emeritus, Harvard Medical School.

The demographic shift toward extended longevity has led to a commensurate increase in the length of the working and productive years, and with it an increase in the number of so-called “older” patients who come into psychoanalysis or psychoanalytic psychotherapy. This led the Psychoanalytic Research and Development Fund to support a four-year study by a small group of experienced psychoanalysts of “the older patient in psychoanalysis.” Aging and old age are considered to be the final developmental crisis of the successive epigenetic phases of the life cycle. Eleven case presentations and various briefer clinical vignettes informed us that older and even old individuals, who seek treatment themselves or who are appropriately referred, respond in a positive and effective fashion to psychoanalysis and psychoanalytic psychotherapy. As do younger patients in analysis, such older people, motivated by the wish to make the most of what time is left, become interested in understanding their pasts with respect to the present and the future. The life stories of two older men who undertook analysis, and the courses and outcomes of their analyses, illustrate our findings and impressions. In both cases conflicts and difficulties from earlier developmental phases, extending back even as far as the oedipal and preoedipal years, were revived during their older years. And although these conflicts might have been beyond definitive resolution, what is salient is the extent to which they were ameliorated: sufficiently that they did not impede a satisfactory adaptive solution of the final crisis of the normative sequences of the life cycle.

[[RunningHead]]

Love in the Therapeutic Alliance

Jack Novick and Kerry Novick

Kerry Novick

617 Stratford Drive
Ann Arbor, MI 48104
Fax: 734-665-2875
E-mail: jnovick@umich.edu

Kerry Novick  

Volumes have been written about the patient's love for the therapist, but there has been relatively little discussion of the therapist's love for the patient. In an attempt to create a theoretical and technical space for discussing the appropriateness and role of love in the therapeutic relationship, a revised concept of the therapeutic alliance is applied to provide technical guidelines and understanding of two kinds of love between patient and therapist, corresponding to two systems of self-esteem regulation: an open, reality-oriented system and a closed, sadomasochistic system organized according to omnipotent beliefs. Examples of the role of love through the phases of treatment illustrate the interrelationship of love and the accomplishment of therapeutic alliance tasks.

responseSet.title: A Semiotic Model of Mind
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Semiotic Model of Mind

David Olds

David Olds

2211 Broadway #IH
New York, NY 10024
Fax: 212-362-3860
E-mail: Ddol@columbia.edu

David Olds   Training and Supervising Analyst, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Columbia College of Physicians and Surgeons.

A theory of signs is presented to arrive at a model of mind that provides a smooth transition from inanimate matter to the thinking brain. Principles of information theory and semiotics are invoked to create a conceptual scheme that can contribute to an understanding of the “mind-body problem.” The thesis is pursued that in living systems, as opposed to inorganic ones, there occurs the phenomenon of semiotic transmission of information. The result is a “dualistic-materialist” position; the dualism arises from the fact that at the beginning of life a set of processes comes into being different from those of the inorganic world. This model has implications for psychology and psychoanalysis. It allows for semiotic systems at different levels—e.g., the molecular, the neural network, the language system, and higher mental functions—to be integrated. Analytic concepts such as free association, clinical technique, feedback systems, personality structure, transference, and repetition compulsions can be understood in both biological and semiotic terms. This model interdigitates with linguistic studies already done in psychoanalysis, as well as with biological models extrinsic to the field.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Adam and Eve Story as Exemplar of an Early-Life Variant of the Oedipus Complex

Marvin Osman

Marvin Osman

9735 Wilshire Boulevard
Suite 446
Beverly Hills, CA 90212
Fax: 310-275-3969
E-mail: Marpaosman@aol.com

Marvin Osman   Training and Supervising Analyst, Southern California Psychoanalytic Institute; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry Emeritus, University of Southern California Medical School.

The Adam and Eve story is construed as having an organizing function that facilitates the analytic understanding of certain patients. In this interpretation, the story epitomizes a psychodynamic in which progressive growth, with separation and individuation, of the young is experienced as perilous—not only to them, but also correspondingly to their procreators. In the myth, the increasing psychic and physical maturation of Adam and Eve produced a crisis. Not only was divine authority flouted, but also apprehensions were aroused that God might be humbled or diminished. This threatened him, evoking his wrath and leading to the punishment by abandonment of his youthful wards. It is suggested that the story depicts an emotional complex of widespread application and is an archaic version of the oedipus complex, continuous with the oedipus complex proper, but from an earlier stage of development. This archaic complex is delineated with clinical vignettes, and a clinical explication of its various components is provided. Clinical management is considered, particularly with reference to the challenge that a closed-system perspective (Fairbairn 1958) presents to a patient's development.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Hannibal

Thomas Harris. New York: Delacorte Press, 1999, 486 pp., $27.95 hardcover, $7.99 paperback

Richard Gottlieb

Richard Gottlieb

263 West End Avenue, # IE
New York, NY 10023
Fax: 212-712-9496
E-mail: Rmgottlieb@aol.com

Richard Gottlieb  

With this novel, Hannibal, Thomas Harris has unwittingly set a feast for psychoanalysts. Although Hannibal has much to teach us, unlocking its secrets requires a key whose simplicity has been clouded by the history within psychoanalysis of the notion of cannibalistic fantasy.

Freud first introduced the idea of cannibalism as a stage of libidinal organization in 1915, in a revision of the Three Essays on the Theory of Sexuality (1905). With this idea he intended to characterize the sexual striving of a neonate and draw attention to its influences on later development. The infant “easts” its mother's breast to the accompaniment of intense pleasure; later on lovers kiss, enjoying a minor pleasure preparatory to the greater one of sexual intercourse. These are old ideas, but they have left their traces on our thinking. One trace is that cannibalistic fantasy has been understood in unduly limited ways. The emphasis in the analytic study of fantasy has been on cannibalism as an act (such as biting, chewing, or swallowing) or inchoate urge—Shengold (1989) writes of aggression of cannibalistic intensity—not as a story.

The fact is that cannibalistic fantasy is involved whenever another person is treated as food (for what is likely the first published statement of this principle, see Malcove 1933). This fact is the key that gains us admittance to the feast that is Harris's Hannibal. Harris presents us an abundance of stories in which persons are treated as food. His characters are baited, hunted, stalked, pursued as prey, killed, skinned, disemboweled, butchered, cooked, seasoned, and relished. Yet there is not much biting, chewing, or swallowing in Hannibal. In this novel, as in the associations of our adult analytic patients, cannibalistic fantasies are expressed through the fully developed capacities of their author. As with all fantasy, the relative richness of their language and imagination, rhythm and cadence, poetry and wit, ambiguity and whimsy, will contribute to the degree of our engagement with the stories being told. And here, it must be said, Harris is a master. Further, his stories are sufficiently representative of the cannibalistic stories our patients tell us that, in my view, they merit study and appreciation.

Commentary

Arnold Wilson

Arnold Wilson

300 Mercer Street, #20C
New York, NY 10003
Fax: 973-746-7986
E-mail: Drarnoldwilson@cs.com

Arnold Wilson  

In this discussion I will elaborate three general points: (1) psychoanalytic writing on the case study, to its detriment, has heretofore overlooked the stream of thought called pragmatism; (2) the case history is the vehicle par excellence for approaching and solving problems inherent in clinical work, and this is true for psychoanalysis as well as other applied disciplines, such as law (see Bromley 1986); (3) this plenary address by Michels is virtually a manifesto of pragmatism, though it does not identify itself as such.

Psychoanalysts have productively tackled the implications of several prevailing trends in current thought, such as postmodernism, the linguistic turn in the social sciences, and the rise of cognitive neuroscience. It is puzzling why the remarkable revival of pragmatism has escaped our notice, since this movement has become something of a cause célèbre. There is at least one proposal on the table suggesting that contemporary pragmatism can be an integrative alternative to two rather loud voices raised in the ongoing culture wars. The proposal, briefly stated, is that we combine the epistemological insights and value awareness of certain threads of postmodernism with the methodological and conceptual achievements of the positivist paradigm (Fishman 1999). The turn to pragmatism may also help untangle a tension in certain psychoanalytic writings on the case study prior to Michel's paper. Those writings, by no accident, stem from these same seemingly diametrical poles. On the one hand, Edelson's bid (1988)

[[RunningHead]]

Supervision as an Intersubjective Process

Hearing from Candidates and Supervisors

Lee Jaffe

Lee Jaffe

3262 Holiday Court
Suite 210
La Jolla, CA 92037-1807
Fax: 858-452-5747
E-Mail: LeeJaffe@compuserve.com

Lee Jaffe  

This panel included two advanced candidates, Katherine Fraser and Joan Wheelis, each of whom presented intersubjective aspects of their supervisory experiences with control cases, and two training analysts, Jay Greenberg and David Sachs, who discussed the candidates' papers. Elizabeth Lloyd Mayer introduced the panel by pointing out that Fraser and Wheelis are experienced supervisees, and by applauding their candor for so openly presenting aspects of the supervisory experience that rarely are discussed in public. To start off, Mayer told the story of a candidate who kept certain aspects of the control analysis from the supervisor because of a clinical disagreement between them. She went on to describe the reality that at times candidates do opt to exclude the supervisor from aspects of the control analysis because they fear (rightly or wrongly) that disagreements with the supervisor may impede their progression.

The first presenter, Fraser, read a paper titled “What Goes On in Supervision and Isn't Talked About: Beyond the Didactic to the Dyadic.” She began with her conviction that the supervisory experience, which she defined as an “opportunity for the student to speak openly and privately over a period of time with an experienced teacher,” is often more troubled than is commonly understood or reported. She stressed the importance of informal discussion among candidates concerning their supervisory experiences, because she has found there are few formal channels for such discussion.

Panel held at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December 21, 1996.

Panelists: Elizabeth Lloyd Mayer (chair), Katherine Fraser, Jay Greenberg, David Sachs, Joan Wheelis. Discussant: Lee Jaffe.

[[RunningHead]]

The Mechanism of Action of Psychoanalytic Treatment

Jon Ellman

Jon Ellman

1650 Cedar Avenue
Montreal, Quebec H3G IA4
Canada
Fax: 514-934-8284

Jon Ellman  

Brian Robertson opened the panel by imparting a sense of urgency to the proceedings. He spoke of the pressing need for research into the therapeutic action of psychoanalysis, not only to allow analysts “to convert clinical beliefs into psychoanalytic knowledge,” but to better answer the challenges of detractors from academia and the market-driven health care system. Even if analysts agree the method works, Robertson continued, “how can we demonstrate this to friend and foe?”

Any mechanism of action must be based on a theory of change. To date, there is no one vision about change. In keeping with theoretical pluralism, any useful model of change will embody multiple causality. Here, Robertson cited Michels's distillation of three overarching theories of change: insight, intensity of emotional experience, and the new relationship with a new object.

Robertson continued by saying that analysts who stress insight also stress interpretation. “Clearly a belief in the power of truth and knowledge is implicit here.” Those who stress intensity of experience believe that rigidly held beliefs, buttressed by rigid intrapsychic structures—created in part by early trauma and fashioned from emergency compromise formations—are shaken and changed by powerful affects. Cognitive scientists argue that a certain affective arousal is important for consolidation of new experience in the mind/brain.

Robertson stated further that analysts who believe in the crucial nature of the interaction stress the experience of both partners in the analytic relationship.

Panel held at the Spring Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Toronto, May 29, 1998.

Panelists: Brian M. Robertson (chair), Lewis Aron, Peter Fonagy, Steven T. Levy. Discussant: Robert Michels.

[[RunningHead]]

The Lobo-Cabernite Affair

Robert Wallerstein

Robert Wallerstein

290 Beach Road
Belvedere, CA 94920
Fax: 415-789-9530

Robert Wallerstein  

March 17, 2000. Helena Besserman Vianna is an outstandingly courageous person. Her willingness to publicly expose Leão Cabernite's egregious behavior as a training analyst in Rio de Janeiro during the military dictatorship—his covering up the nefarious conduct of his training analysand Amilcar Lobo, a military psychiatrist complicit in the torture being inflicted on political prisoners (his role being to keep the torture victims alive so that the questioning could continue)—and to do this at real risk of arrest and worse, has properly won the admiration of her informed psychoanalytic colleagues, both in Brazil and around the world. It therefore saddens me that her book recounting her experience of this shameful episode in psychoanalytic history—psychoanalysis, Dictatorship, and Torture: Don't Talk About It—is indeed replete with misunderstanding and distortion of the efforts of the IPA and its elected officers, as I have known these efforts, to bring an honorable conclusion to the strife and turmoil that has characterized the Psychoanalytic Society of Rio de Janeiro, (SPRJ), also known as Rio I, for over three decades now, and to remove Cabernite from participation in that society.

I have already responded twice to reviews of Besserman's book, once in the January 1999 Bulletin of the British Psychoanalytical Society to a review by Peter Hildebrand, and once in JAPA (47/3) to a review by Janine Puget. I felt that both reviews had repeated, unchallenged, some of what I felt were unfounded allegations impugning the role of the IPA and its elected leaders in the handling of the Cabernite affair. Besserman wrote a rebuttal to my commentary in the Bulletin, which was published along with my response in the March 1999 issue. She has now written a rebuttal to my commentary in JAPA, and the editors have asked that I write a response to that. In this response I will limit myself to statements made by Besserman in this issue of JAPA; those interested in a fuller version of these unhappy events are referred to the sources just noted.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Self-Criticism and the Psychic Surface

Lawrence Josephs

Lawrence Josephs

Garden City, NY 11530

Lawrence Josephs  

It is postulated that the surface of the mind reflects a variety of shifting self-syntonic and self-dystonic contents striving to achieve a stable homeostatic balance. Shifts between self-syntonic and self-dystonic contents are seen to reflect an underlying intrasystemic conflict within the superego between conflicting superego injunctions. These shifts possess a cyclical quality, as self-syntonic contents become self-dystonic and as self-dystonic contents become self-syntonic. This conceptualization of the psychic surface has implications for a comparative study of psychoanalytic technique and for implementing the technical recommendation to work from surface to depth. It is suggested that the analyst's interpretations become assimilated within cyclical processes of superego reaction and repair.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Treatment of the Older Adult

The Impact on the Psychoanalyst

Frieda Plotkin

Frieda Plotkin

241 Central Park West, Suite IA
New York, NY 10024
Fax: 212-787-4946
E-mail: Frieda.plotkin@usa.net

Frieda Plotkin  

Work with the elderly challenges analysts in special ways. The author presents clinical material from her practice and from investigative interviews with nine treating analysts who report personal reactions and countertransferences to analytic work with twelve elderly patients. She concludes that the major challenges in work with the elderly come more from the analyst than from the patients. Issues arising from the analyst's unresolved feelings about aging, parents, loss, and death are revived in the treatment of the elderly in an especially intense form. In this paper many aspects of transference are considered, especially those relating to illness, loss, and the problems that flow from identifications of the older analyst with the patient. The affective reverberations in younger and older analysts with regard to the initiation of analytic treatment, the inevitability of moving in and out of real life crises, the sustaining of loss, and, particularly, the impending termination, are presented in the paper.

responseSet.authorCitation: Aruffo, R. N., Ibarra, S. & Strupp, K. R.
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Encopresis and Anal Masturbation

Roy Aruffo , Segundo Ibarra and Karen Strupp

Karen Strupp

5300 San Jacinto, #105
Houston, TX 77004
Fax: 713-523-0941
Email: Raruffo@swbell.net

Karen Strupp   is Assistant Professor of Psychiatry, Baylor College of Medicine.

Current pediatric and psychiatric studies on encopresis and its treatment are heavily influenced by mechanical, physiological, and behavioral considerations. Although psychodynamic treatment has generally been considered to be of little benefit, and its findings suspect, the authors suggest that a psychodynamic approach adds substantially to the understanding of some cases of encopresis; that the anal sensations and anal erotic feelings reported by a number of encopretic children are intense, and that the encopretic symptom, soiling, in these children is the result of a conscious form of anal masturbation in which the fecal mass is used for stimulation; and that any study of encopresis is incomplete that does not include what encopretic children, engaged in a sound therapeutic relationship, know and say about their soiling. The authors further suggest that physical treatments of those children whose encopresis is psychologically driven may be contraindicted. The presence of a large stool does not in itself substantiate a physical illness. Further research is needed to elucidate the prevalence of anal masturbation in encopretic children.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Finding the Neutral Position

Patient and Analyst Perspectives

Ronald Baker

Ronald Baker

12 Southwood Lawn Road
Highgate
London N6 5SF
England
E-mail: Ronaldbaker@clara.co.uk

Ronald Baker  

In light of current debates between classical and intersubjective schools of psychoanalysis, the challenge posed by the latter to such basic concepts as the analyst's neutrality, anonymity, and abstinence is taken up. It is maintained that the term neutral position is today more germane and meaningful than the term neutrality, which frequently has been taken to prescribe the analyst's posture. It is proposed that for each patient the neutral position is uniquely sited and that it is incumbent on the analyst to find its location. The neutral position is defined within the context of the interaction between analyst and patient. The concept is therefore compatible with—indeed it is essential to—an intersubjective or relational orientation. The manifold reasons, conscious and unconscious, why the analyst is vulnerable to leaving the neutral position are considered. The patient's reaction to the analyst who has left the neutral position and the analyst's clinical use of this reaction are discussed.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Overstimulated State of Dyslexia

Perception, Knowledge, and Learning

Sydney Arkowitz

Sydney Arkowitz

1601 N. Tucson Blvd. #21
Tucson, AZ 85716
Fax: 520-297-1228
E-mail: Arkowitz@ahsc.arizona.edu

Sydney Arkowitz   Training and Supervising Analyst, Southwest Center for Psychoanalytic Studies.

Dyslexia is far more than a learning disorder; it has significant impact on personality organization. While dyslexia usually begins to manifest most clearly in early latency when the challenge of learning to read is at its height, often the dyslexic child's ego development and functioning has already been adversely affected. The literature from neuropsychology suggests that dyslexia is a subtle language-processing disorder that affects emotional, cognitive, and social development. The neuroanatomical literature also suggests a significant correlation between the neurodevelopmental basis for dyslexia, the caregiving environment, and psychological development. These two bodies of literature and analytic observations of a dyslexic patient suggest that the dyslexic individual may have a neurological deficit that increases vulnerability to overstimulation. The author hypothesizes that emotional and cognitive states result and reappear within the analytic encounter. This complicates clinical assessment and technical decisions. The author presents an analytic case and examines (1) the impact of deficit on the development of conflict; (2) the impact of the overwhelmed ego on the mastery of developmental tasks; and (3) the impact of dyslexia on dysgnosia, transference, and analytic process and technique.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the Vicissitudes of Early Primary Surrogate Mothering II

Loss of the Surrogate Mother and Arrest of Mourning

Harry Hardin and Daniel Hardin

Daniel Hardin

2900 Yonge Street, Suite 101
Toronto, Ontario M4N 3N8
Canada
Fax: 416-483-5306
E-mail: Hthardin@sympatico.ca

Daniel Hardin   Sociologist. Submitted for publication August 10, 1998

Among the catastrophic events experienced by infants and young children, one of the most frequent is the loss of an early primary surrogate mother (EPSM). Usually permanent, the loss is often followed by the advent of a new, “replacement” caregiver. One aspect of the emotional environment is unique to this kind of caregiving situation: that parents are often unable to validate the true nature of their child's relationship with the EPSM or, ultimately, the trauma experienced by the child when the EPSM leaves. The marked discrepancy between the parent's and the infant or child's experience of the surrogate mothering leads to an arrest of the child's mourning process, with the potential for serious developmental consequences. Issues related to EPSM loss and its aftermath are examined in the light of two examples. Further exploration of the environment of this kind of caregiving directs attention to the critical need to nurture and protect the attachments of both the infant or child and the parent to the ESPM.

[[RunningHead]]

Commentary

Imre Szecsödy

Imre Szecsödy

Karlavägen 27
S-114 31 Stockholm
Sweden
Fax:+46-8-662-1161
E-mail: Imre.szecsody@mip.ki.se

Imre Szecsödy  

It is both an honor and a challenge to respond to Robert Michels's comprehensive and interesting paper. “Psychoanalysis,” he begins, “is based on theories and on clinical data, the events that transpire between patients and analysts in the consultation room.” He then points to the scarcity of extensive case reports, noting that “the clinical data in our literature are more and more likely to consist of vignettes or snapshots, rather than full-length accounts.” Michels emphasizes the importance of comprehending “the psychoanalytic process as a whole, the story of the unfolding of the patient-analyst relationship and its influence on everything that transpires in the analysis.” He organizes his discussion around five perspectives: (1) the nature and (2) the purpose of case reports, (3) oral vs. written reports, (4) the relation between case reports and analyses, and (5) how the case report offers a special vista on the analysis itself, “revealing the analyst's awareness and comfort vis-à-vis his or her intentions” and conveying “more than the analyst knows,” as it is “at least in part a countertransference theme or enactment.” It is easy to follow his text, as he makes his point clearly. His conclusion is: “I find it far more enlightening to have analysts tell as best they can why they want to tell us anything at all, and then to weave an account of those intentions into their account of an analysis. The most meticulous reproductions of the most detailed data, dissociated from the context of why it was selected, for what audience, and for what purpose, is like an isolated electron microscope image separated from identification of the tissue, the organ, the species, or the stain—a demonstration of method isolated from scientific relevance.” This is what I wish to focus on, the aspect of the purpose of case reports. I will emphasize the necessity of opening windows on the analytic process.

Understanding, sharing of experience, introspection, and empathy are prerequisites for psychoanalytic treatment, but objectifying methods of some sort are indispensable correctives (Thomà and Kàchele 1975). Analysts have an emotional need to do their work in a way that conforms with their personality, and each creates in the consulting room a uniquely individual atmosphere. The analyst constructs a patient's psychic reality within the framework of the psychoanalytic theory he or she prefers. Sydney Pulver (1987), in a fascinating discussion of clinical data presented by Martin Silverman, makes this very point: “An analyst's theoretical orientation has a marked impact on the way he thinks about patients and the way he works with them” (p. 289).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Body Ego and the Preodipal Roots of Feminine Gender Identity

Richard Lasky

Richard Lasky

257 Central Park West
New York, NY 10024
Fax: 212-579-1733

Richard Lasky   Training and Control Analyst and Faculty, Institute for Psychoanalytic Training and Research; Clinical Professor of Psychology and Supervising Analyst, New York University Postdoctoral Program

Traditionally, visual cues (that is, seeing the difference between one's own genitals and those of the opposite sex) and the fantasies that they prompt were thought to be the central determinants in the organization of gender identity. More recently attention has been focused on mother-infant interaction patterns, on the construction of the body itself, on its kinesthetic sensations, and on the fantasies that these evoke. These matters are now thought to be equal in importance to the traditional considerations. The resulting concept of a core gender identity has suggested the possibility of various forms of preoedipal genital anxiety. This paper discusses how two experiences common to childhood as well as adulthood—the tumescence of erectile tissue and the involuntary vaginal secretions of sexual arousal—may contribute to the organization of feminine gender identity. Case material, including a close examination of an analytic hour, is presented to show how this process may manifest itself in adult neurotic conflict.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Spontaneity Versus Constraint

Dilemmas in the Analyst's Decision Making

Charles Brice

Charles Brice

4716 Ellsworth Avenue
#719 Pittsburgh, PA 15213

Charles Brice  

Joseph Schachter opened the panel by reminding those present that spontaneity and restraint stand in dialectical relation to each other and have been present in our discipline from the beginning. He defined spontaneity as characterizing those acts of the analyst that are unplanned, involuntary, impulsive, and without premeditation; restraint as characterizing those that are planned, deliberate, and voluntary.

Noting that both Freud and Merton Gill believed that neutrality does not proscribe spontaneity, Schachter outlined the advantages and disadvantages of both relational possibilities. The analyst's spontaneity may express authenticity, facilitate intimacy, and make the analyst more accessible as an object of identification. However, spontaneity can also be disastrous, as when an analyst unthinkingly burdens a patient with his or her own needs or desires. The analyst's restraint maintains the boundaries that support the authority necessary for the analyst to maintain neutrality and promote the patient's autonomy. Restraint may, however, inhibit intimacy and fail to protect the patient from the analyst's subtle communication of judgments and preferences.

First stating that the analyst must consciously or unconsciously choose one or the other response, but not both simultaneously, and that he or she is responsible for the choice made, Schachter posed a number of questions for the panel to consider. Is our capacity to modify our behavior with patients enhanced if we learn under what conditions we become spontaneous or restrained?

Panel held at the Spring Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, San Diego, May 17, 1997.

Panelists: Joseph Schachter (chair), Ronald Fleischmann, Diane Martinez, Ana-Maria Rizzuto.

549

[[RunningHead]]

Commentary

Marcia Cavell

Marcia Cavell

1570 Olympus Avenue
Berkeley, CA 94708
Fax: 510-548-5110
E-mail: Cavell@uclink4.berkeley.edu

Marcia Cavell  

It is an honor to be asked to respond to Warren Poland's fine paper, “Witnessing and Otherness.” I have thought quite a bit about the second half of his subject, less about what he calls witnessing, which I had assumed to be an essential aspect of empathic listening. But I think he is right: witnessing is sufficiently painful for the analyst, and important for the patient, to deserve recognition on its own. Let me begin with “otherness.”

48

It is an ordinary, undeniable fact that although you can have pain at the same spot along your sciatic nerve as I have along mine, you cannot have my pain. In fact, you and I can have the “same” pain only by having different pains. Subject our two bodies to stimulation of just the same nerve fibers, and you and I will still be suffering our separate pains. It is another such fact that you cannot know my mind in quite the way you can sometimes know your own. Nor can you read my mind in the way you read the pages of a book, for one thing because minds are not visible in just the way that books are. And it is yet another that you cannot see the world from exactly my perspective, that is, the perspective defined not only by my exact position in space and time, but also by my memories, my associations, and so on. I call these facts and others in the same family “the facts of separateness.”

We often preface our words of advice by saying “If I were you…”; but of course, no matter how entwined our paths, I never will be, can be, you. Even coming as close as one can to being another is so demanding that we seldom do it; it requires both acknowledging the ways in which you and I are similar—and therefore vulnerable to many of the same sorts of pain—and honoring the differences between us. The acknowledgment of similarities is difficult because it means recognizing that with a turn of fortune I too could be overwhelmed with shame, or beside myself with terror, as you are; that only by luck and for the moment am I in a position less painful than yours; that no transcending power exempts me from the human condition. Honoring our differences is difficult because it requires imagination on my part, and tolerance for the fact of difference itself.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Melting the Darkness: The Dyad and Principles of Clinical Practice

Warren S. Poland. Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson, 1996, xvi + 310 pp., $45.00.

Michael Gundle

Michael Gundle

2910 E. Madison Street
Seattle, WA 98112
Fax: 206-726-6634
E-mail: Mikejg@u.washington.edu

Michael Gundle   faculty, Seattle Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; Clinical Associate Professor of Psychiatry, University of Washington.

“The Analyst at Work” is the title of one of the sections of this fine book. That would have made an apt, if less poetic, title for the complete work. What comes across throughout this volume is an analyst devoted to his craft, one who is more than happy to discuss with the reader the way he works, thinks, and feels with his patients. So consistent is Poland's approach to his work that, although these chapters are revisions of papers published over the past twenty-five years, I could not tell from the contents of each chapter which was written earlier and which later. Like a patient silversmith, Poland is endlessly fascinated with both his tools and his material—his patients. That fascination is contagious.

The dyad referred to in the subtitle is the clinical psychoanalytic dyad, analyst and patient. Poland considers a number of aspects of the workings of this dyad: the analytic surface, transference, insight, the analyst's neutrality, the analyst's words, the analyst's empathy, the analyst's tact, laughter, long analyses, and self-analysis. Regardless of which aspect he is discussing, the dyad is always the focus. I appreciated the abundance of clinical examples and their clarity. They are what make this book of value. Poland is not much concerned with theory. He is conversant with the analytic literature, but he is interested mainly in describing what works for him, practically, with live patients.

The debate between advocates of one-person and two-person psychologies has been a prominent feature of our meetings and journals for the past twenty years. Small forests have undoubtedly been sacrificed to publish the arguments on either side. Poland makes few apparent references to this debate. What he does, which I found valuable, is to demonstrate in his clinical examples his own resolution of that controversy. Poland takes the approach that both perspectives are of value and are in constant dialectical interaction. He is a living example of Wallerstein's position: “though two psychic worlds inevitably mix and fuse in analytic work, each influencing the other… only one history, one psychology, is the focal object of the analytic scrutiny…” (Wallerstein 1998, p. 1031). Poland moves effortlessly from attending to the patient's intrapsychic conflicts and defenses to actively listening to his responses to them and then attending once more to the patient.

[[RunningHead]]

Deepening the Treatment

Jane S. Hall. Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson, 1998, 214 pp., $40.00

Alma Bond

Alma Bond

606 Truman Avenue
Key West, FL 33040
Fax: 305-292-6457

Alma Bond   Fellow and faculty member, Institute for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

Deepening the Treatment is an interesting book that reads like a novel. Its author, Jane Hall, is intense, dedicated, and largely orthodox in her technique. She is, I suspect, a fine analyst. She obviously loves her work and believes in her craft, and communicates this to her patients and students. Her book is an excellent text for the fledgling analyst. Unfortunately, while everything she writes is true (oh, how true!), she

305

has little to say to the experienced practitioner. “This book,” she writes, “is meant to assist the psychoanalytically informed therapist in thinking about interpreting transference, countertransference, resistance, dreams, fantasies, and actions while helping her work in concert with the patient who will increasingly take over these interpreting functions” (pp. 1-2). To me, her “psychoanalytically informed therapist” does not sound so very “informed.” Nevertheless, the book is recommended reading for students of psychotherapy, and should be assigned reading for beginners' classes.

While an ardent believer in the rule of abstinence in treatment, Hall contends that theory is secondary to the person, and that useful as it is, “theory must become the ground to the figure of the patient” (p. 47). She quotes her longtime supervisor, Martin Nass, to the effect that “the patient writes the theory” (p. 9). For example, Hall will often accept an individual on a once-a-week basis if he or she is not yet ready for analysis. In a nice turn of phrase, she says many people coming for weekly therapy “often have little idea that their presenting problems are just the first few bars of their song” (p. 2) and that “people often need to wade rather than dive directly into deep water” (p. 3).

In an orthodox manner, Hall does not believe in answering questions in analysis, even on such matters as to where she is going on vacation. According to her, “answering questions takes away the patient's right to wonder and to explore her own fantasies” (p. 15). She sticks to this principle even though at times the patient has not returned to treatment after the vacation. Perhaps here was an instance where Hall needed to remember her dictum that theory is secondary to the person.

[[RunningHead]]

Diversity and Direction in Psychoanalytic Technique

Fred Pine. New Haven: Yale University Press, 1998, viii + 234 pp., $25.00

Jacob Jacobson

Jacob Jacobson

1636 16th Street
Boulder, CO 80302
Fax: 303-440-9222
E-mail: JGJ1234@aol.com

Jacob Jacobson   Training and Supervising Analyst, Denver Institute for Psychoanalysis; Clinical Professor, Department of Psychiatry, University of Colorado Health Sciences Center.

With this book, Fred Pine carries forward his work of the past ten years on the “Four Psychologies” (drive, ego, object relations, and self), now focusing on the realm of psychoanalytic technique. As a caveat by way of Truth in Reviewing, I will disclose at the outset that, influenced by

308

my patients and by pioneers of the multiple models approach like Pine, my own current viewpoint is quite congruent with his.

Not merely accepting the diversity of current psychoanalytic points of view as a fact, Pine clearly also celebrates it, in the belief that patients are better served by a flexible technique that entertains diverse possibilities. He argues that as psychoanalytic theory has evolved from its beginnings as a drive theory, through its phases of ego psychology, object relations, and self theories, each stage has retained its relevance, rather than having been superseded. His clinical examples illustrate his argument that “a broadened theoretical perspective provides the conceptual basis for a more balanced technique, shaped to the individual patient and the particular clinical moment” (p. 51). He makes no excuse for his multiple models position: “If we require multiple models… in order to hear patients adequately, then that is simply what the work entails” (p. 154). He argues that arriving at a particular intervention from within a four psychologies model is no more difficult than from within a unified theory. In either case, the specifics are arrived at by an empathic sense of the patient's apparent clinical needs at the given moment. No theory, unitary or otherwise, tells the clinician precisely how to address which aspect, or when to do it. In fact, Pine wonders, as do I, whether in their clinical work holders of a unitary theory may at moments quietly use other models in an adaptive way without being aware they are doing so.

In assessing the relative importance of interpretation and the relationship, Pine views them not as an either/or dichotomy, but rather as interweaving with each other, especially poignantly at moments of interpretation. He notes the importance of the analyst's being sensitive to the possibility that various of the ordinarily neutral, nonspecific relational attributes of the psychoanalytic situation—reliability, attentive nonjudgmental listening, etc.—may be experienced as specifically transformative by some patients (e.g., being listened to, for a patient for whom that is a new experience) or as traumatizing by others (e.g., silence, for a patient who finds silence disorganizing).

[[RunningHead]]

Relational Psychoanalysis: The Emergence of a Tradition

Stephen A. Mitchell and Lewis Aron. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 1999, 496 pp., $37.50 paperback.

Glen Gabbard

Glen Gabbard

P.O. Box 829
Topeka, KS 66601-0829
Fax: 785-272-9577
E-mail: Gabbargo@menninger.edu

Glen Gabbard  

In the last two decades of the twentieth century the relational movement gave American psychoanalysis a much needed shot in the arm. The borders of this movement were not clearly circumscribed, and the contributors to relational thinking did not always think of themselves as relational theorists. Nevertheless, despite the somewhat amorphous shape of the burgeoning movement, relational thinking has been instrumental in creating an intellectual renaissance in American psychoanalysis. The challenges posed by relational analysts have caused most American psychoanalysts to rethink their positions on major issues, and the result has been perhaps the most exciting era in which to be an analyst in a long, long time.

Stephen A. Mitchell and Lewis Aron have been two of the leading lights in the relational movement, and it was their splendid idea to bring together in one volume a number of its seminal contributions. They have succeeded admirably and have produced a superb starting point for analysts wishing to familiarize themselves with the core concepts of relational thinking. The book could also serve as a text for a seminar in relational psychoanalysis.

In their preface the editors assist the reader by mapping out the territory subsumed under the relational rubric. They acknowledge debts to self psychology, intersubjectivity theory, social constructivism, interpersonal psychoanalysis, object relations theory, hermeneutics, feminist psychoanalytic writing, and the work of Ferenczi. They also clarify that relational psychoanalysts do not address phenomena different from those explored by the drive/defense model; rather, they offer alternative understandings of the same phenomena.

315

[[RunningHead]]

Commentary

Stephen Bernstein

Stephen Bernstein

146 Wallis Road
Chestnut Hill, MA 02467
Fax: 617-327-8385
E-mail: Sbb@massmed.org

Stephen Bernstein  

We have not yet developed a generally accepted and convincing method of writing about our clinical work that would allow us to reveal it to colleagues or show others the unique and powerful treatment that is psychoanalysis. We have not often found an effective method of translation from the familiar, private oral mode in which the analysis takes place into the more public written mode while yet retaining the “music” of the analytic interaction. Robert Michels addresses some of these difficulties in his comprehensive “history of the case history.” He reviews and examines the historical, political, and dynamic forces that have shaped and impeded the development of the analytic case report.

Michels's central thesis concerns what he calls “intention” in case histories. That is, he believes that case reports are written in a certain context, for a particular purpose, and that this purpose is revealed in the text either explicitly or implicitly. Michels would like writers to become aware of these intentions and to share them more openly with readers, because he believes that such candor will shed light on the analyst and on the analytic process.

In an effort to frame his thesis—that we can read to greater advantage by observing the context or the analyst's intention in writing a case history—Michels contrasts two views of how clinical material should be reported.

381

Philip Rubovits-Seitz

Philip Rubovits-Seitz

2623 Woodley Place NW
Washington, DC 20008
E-mail: Rrs2623@aol.com

Philip Rubovits-Seitz  

This discussion presents arguments for two proposed improvements in psychoanalytic case reports—namely, more frequent reports of the whole analysis, and greater emphasis on the presentation of clinical evidence. These two suggested improvements are related, because an important source of clinical evidence—the justification of interpretations—draws on the data of the whole analysis.

Early in his essay, Michels notes that our clinical literature consists largely of vignettes rather than full-length accounts of the therapeutic process, and he asks why this is so. In one of his writings, Freud (1918) asserted that reporting a complete history is both “technically impractical” and “socially impermissible,” and would in any event be “unconvincing” (p. 8). Elsewhere, however (Freud 1905), he concluded that an “intelligent, consistent and unbroken case history” is possible only at the end of a treatment; that completed cases offer the advantage of hindsight; and that definitive interpretation of any fragment must await completion of the whole analysis (p. 18)—in short, “the whole analysis is needed to explain it” (Freud 1911, p. 93; see also Schafer 1986, p. 156; Goldberg 1997, p. 437). Thus, the preparation of a case report is best postponed until the analysis is over.

In his report of the Wolf Man, Freud (1918) referred to still another advantage of waiting until the end of an analysis before writing a report of the case. He indicated that all of the information that made it possible to understand the patient's neurosis “was derived from the last period of the work, during which resistance temporarily disappeared and the patient gave an impression of lucidity which is usually attainable only in hypnosis” (p.11; for similar views on the clarification of pathodynamics during later stages of analysis, see French 1958

[[RunningHead]]

Lawrence Friedman and the Trouble with “Reality”

David Pincus

David Pincus

12429 Cedar Road Suite 2
Cleveland Heights, OH 44106
E-mail: Dpincus216@aol.com

David Pincus  

August 23, 1999. I have read Lawrence Friedman's beautifully written and “highly partisan account of a complex intellectual history”—“Why is Reality a Troubling Concept?” (JAPA 47/2)—many times over, and each time I find something new in it. His way of considering the philosophical and psychoanalytic problem with reality from the vantage point of the nominalist/realist distinction is fascinating. While I do not agree with some of his conclusions, his scholarship and clarity inspire me to respond. I would like to make four minor points before turning to my main one.

First, I am pleased that Friedman has pointed out the “bad rap” that positivism suffers in current discourse. People are often criticizing a reductive or distilling enterprise that from a philosophical standpoint should be termed essentialism, not positivism. But somehow positivism takes the rap, and I have always wondered why. My own opinion is that the current emphasis on contextualism takes prisoners of any persons or positions that lay claim to certainty, authority, or some notion of objectivity. “Don't be so sure of yourself, you can't be positive” moves from a judgment or objection to the incorrect labeling of a philosophical enterprise.

Second, I have had a pathological realist (of sorts) and a pathological nominalist (of sorts) in my clinical practice, one treatment hour following the other, resulting in some interesting contrasts. The “realist” is a compulsive categorizer, a premature labeler, an externalizing materialist reductionist of sorts, a very narrow sort of engineer. The work here has been to help him become aware of his dread of uncertainty, fear of loss, and all the rest. The “nominalist,” by contrast, is a postmodern religious scholar who views the treatment as a text that can be read from many standpoints. Points are often taken up in conjecture and multiple perspectives. Here the work is to help him to tolerate more embedded and located issues, in his case (I imagine) a fear of his murderous aggression.

CCRT Not Rorschach-Derived

Lewis Kirshner

Lewis Kirshner

306 Harvard Street
Cambridge, MA 02139-2211

Lewis Kirshner  

November 16, 1999. In my article “Toward a Postmodern Realism for Psychoanalysis” (JAPA 47/2) there was an error. I said that Luborsky's CCRT construct measures “relationship themes derived from the Rorschach test” (p. 453). This was a parapraxis of memory on my part. The methodology of the CCRT has nothing to do with the Rorschach test. This slip did not effect the point I was making about the CCRT and as such was irrelevant to my argument, but it misrepresents the instrument.

600

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Therapy as Health Care: Effectiveness and Economics in the Twenty-First Century

Harriette Kaley, Morris N. Eagle, and David L. Wolitzky. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 1999, 312 pp., $49.95.

Jane Buckwalter

Jane Buckwalter

184 Berkeley Place
Brooklyn, NY 11217
Fax: 718-783-1826
E-mail: Mjanebuc@msn.com

Jane Buckwalter  

Psychoanalytic values, including confidentiality, neutrality, autonomy and self-determination of the patient, and especially the central importance of the treatment relationship, are dramatically opposed to managed care's focus on the quick fix and the profit margin for the next quarter. The editors of this book offer a timely, multifaceted approach to the problems stemming from this polarity, in all forms of psychoanalytically informed treatment.

669

The book is organized into four sections. The first presents an overview of the problems, with suggestions for the future, most chapters having been written for the 1994 American Psychological Association meetings with the theme “Psychoanalysis as Health Care.” A chapter by Bryan Welch describes the belated political awakening of psychologists and psychoanalysts, the gradual public awareness of managed care abuses, and the subsequent legal challenges that are beginning to hold managed care companies more accountable. Dorothy Cantor (mistakenly identified under “Contributors” as a former president of the American Psychoanalytic Association) gives a rousing call for psychologists to become politically active. One need only look at the figures in the Hay Group Study showing that mental health dollars paid by insurance have decreased 54 percent since 1988, while general health dollars declined 7.4 percent (National Association of Psychiatric Health Systems 1998). James Barron, in his chapter, makes the important point that managed care has been able to use the positivistic, antisubjective viewpoint in psychology as an ally in the denial of subjectivity and the unconscious, focusing on target symptoms and excluding underlying characterological or causative issues. While the chapters in this section all present important aspects of the history of managed care, why we got where we are, and where we may be going, the word “psychologist” should have been replaced by “analyst” or “therapist” when these chapters were rewritten for this book. While it may not in fact indicate the views of these writers, the use of “psychologist” here appears to promote the division among our disciplines that has in part occasioned our delay in mobilizing a collective effort.
