Earl Hopper  

July 21, 1999. In order to complete his examination of the theme of orality in The Merchant of Venice, Michael Fleisher (JAPA 47/2) must also explore such topics as (1) the unconscious associations of breast and penis, as well as breast and feces; (2) the feminine connotations of the foreskin (e.g., lips/labia) and the masculine connotations of circumcision as the central element of the Covenant between Abraham and God (Nunberg 1949); and (3) the types of homosexuality represented by Antonio, based primarily on eroticized narcissistic oral and anal introjection of the breast, and by Shylock, based primarily on the erotization of anal and phallic rage toward the father, both types including passive and active processes (Hopper 1995, 1997).

The absence of a nurturing and protective mother is, of course, at the root of these three phenomena. Antonios do not cling excessively to their mother's sails (skirts, slips, etc.) when they have been fed well and sufficiently, and Shylocks are not provoked to make strict demands when they have no need to repudiate their own tender feelings toward their sons, not to mention their jealousy. It is unnecessary to make a meal of this, but mothers often need the authority of fathers in order to protect themselves from being bitten off and “Gobbo-ed” up.

It is worth noting that Shakespeare also treats these themes elsewhere, e.g., in Coriolanus.

Donald Kuspit  

This is a wonderfully jumpy book, as so many conference proceedings are. The conference memorialized here was held at Stanford University in 1991, ostensibly to celebrate Freud's contributions to a psychoanalytic understanding of culture. In fact, however, it allowed various prominent minds to range freely over the cultural terrain, with a psychoanalytic compass in hand, sometimes—but only sometimes—pointing to that supposedly steady North Star, Freud, while otherwise oscillating, often wildly, in the direction of other psychoanalytic stars.

[[RunningHead]]

Ours, Yours, Mine

Mutuality and the Emergence of the Separate Self.

Anni Bergman, in collabration with Maria F. Fahey. Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson, 1999, xxiv + 457 pp., $45.00.

Ruth Fischer

Ruth Fischer

658 Black Rock Road
Bryn Mawr, PA 19010
Fax: 610-581-0510
E-mail: Fischer2@mail.med.upenn.edu

Ruth Fischer   Training and Supervising Analyst in Adult, Adolescent, and Child Psychoanalysis, Philadelphia Psychoanalytic Institute; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, University of Pennsylvania School of Medicine

This is a collection of essays written by Bergman over a twenty-year period and documenting the origin and development of separation-individuation theory. She shows us how the original research that established the subphases of the development of the sense of self and other in the first three years of life evolved into a treatment for psychotic children and a theoretical framework for the understanding of normal development. For all of this and more, Bergman was there. It is only in reading these papers as a unit that one can fully appreciate the depth and breadth of the endeavor.

The book is divided into two sections: “Theory,” and “Clinical Application: The Understanding and Treatment of Psychotic Children.” This is not only a book for those who treat disturbed children. There is much here that is pertinent for the analyst of adults who is interested in attunement and intersubjective resonance.

The theoretical section begins with Mahler's original research into the dawning of the sense of self: the establishment of the sequence of subphases from symbiosis through differentiation, practicing, rapprochement, and the beginning of emotional object constancy. This is a nice synopsis of the basic components of separation-individuation theory. And it is much more. Each subphase is explored, and its specific contribution to the process made clear. Symbiosis establishes a secure base from which the child can differentiate and move out into the world with both curiosity and anxiety. The more secure the base, the greater the comfort and freedom to invest in the world. The practicing subphase, as an opportunity for just such an investment, is explored in depth in terms of mother-child interactions, play, the use of space, and the role of possessions such as toys and clothes. Interactions set down at this time become lifelong patterns.

1637
